PATIENT: GUANGUL ZEKIROS

MRN: 1394784

DOB: 08/28/1956

Date of Service: 10/24/2024

Referring Provider:
Dr. Amar Gupta

Reason for Consultation/Chief Complaint:
Establish care for patient with highly suspected HCC.

Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.

Pathology:
History of Present Illness:
68 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin. 

Today, he presents with his son.  He had been feeling better since discharge from the hospital.  His abdominal pain have subsided but not completely resolved and his and nausea and vomiting had improved.  He is eating better.  No shortness of breath, cough, hemoptysis or chest pain.  No fever, chills.  No prior history of GI bleeding, ascites or hepatic and cephalopathy episodes.

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hepatitis C
BPH

Past Surgical History:

History of inguinal surgery

OB/GYN History:

Imaging:
Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.

Problem List:

	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Abnormal iron profile (finding) ( ICD-10:R79.0 ;Abnormal level of blood mineral )

	Fatigue ( ICD-10:R53.83 ;Other fatigue )
	Gallbladder calculus (disorder) ( ICD-10:K80.20 ;Calculus of gallbladder without cholecystitis without obstruction )
	Malabsorption ( ICD-10:K90.9 ;Intestinal malabsorption, unspecified )
	Thrombocytopenia ( ICD-10:D69.6 ;Thrombocytopenia, unspecified )
	Umbilical hernia (disorder) ( ICD-10:K42.9 ;Umbilical hernia without obstruction or gangrene )

Impression:
68-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC:

HCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hypervascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. He is currently status post left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score is 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. 

I counseled him about my concerns of peritoneal seeding with his rupture and my examination of starting him on systemic therapy for HCC after discharge and clinical stabilization. This is based on the high likelihood of residual disease ( liver and peritoneum), low toxicity profile of therapy, and the encouraging results of EMERALD-1 trial suggesting adding systemic therapy to embolization could be of value among intermediate stage HCC patients

Plan:
Extensive counseling as above
Plan for systemic therapy with immunotherapy as above.

.

Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

Interval History:
Patient presents to start immunotherapy.  Since last seen in clinic, he has been feeling reasonably okay with resolution of his abdominal pain.  He denies any GI bleeding.  Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea or constipation.  His weight, energy and appetite are all stable.  His main complaint is left ear pain associated with decreased hearing acuity.  He reports this has been going well for a couple of months.  It is persistent and is worsening with time.  In the past he had a lot of wax removed from the right ear.  Denies any fever, chills, nausea, vomiting.  No shortness of breath, cough, hemoptysis.  No balance issues.  2 weeks ago he presented emergently to the ER with chest pain.  He had CT angiogram that showed no PE and apparently had EKG and troponin that were negative.  He is currently planned to follow-up with cardiology for stress test.

Impression:
68-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC:

HCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hypervascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. He is currently status post left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score is 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. 

I previously counseled him about my concerns of peritoneal seeding with his rupture and my inclination of starting him on systemic therapy for HCC  based on the high likelihood of residual disease ( liver and peritoneum), low toxicity profile of therapy, and the encouraging results of EMERALD-1 trial suggesting adding systemic therapy to embolization could be of value among intermediate stage HCC patients.  Of note, liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease.

Today, he is clinically well with lab results within acceptable limits.  We will proceed with cycle 1 day 1 tremelimumab durvalumab.

Ear pain:
likely impacted cerumen. pt pain limited ear exam. discussed home remedies including hydrogen peroxide. refer to ENT. 

Atypical anginal pain episode:
none since hospital visit. planning to follow up with cardiology.

Neoplasm related pain:

extracted.patient_update: Patient presents to start immunotherapy. Since last seen in clinic, he has been feeling reasonably okay with resolution of his abdominal pain. He denies any GI bleeding. Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea or constipation. His weight, energy and appetite are all stable. His main complaint is left ear pain associated with decreased hearing acuity. He reports this has been going well for a couple of months. It is persistent and is worsening with time. In the past he had a lot of wax removed from the right ear. Denies any fever, chills, nausea, vomiting. No shortness of breath, cough, hemoptysis. No balance issues. 2 weeks ago he presented emergently to the ER with chest pain. He had CT angiogram that showed no PE and apparently had EKG and troponin that were negative. He is currently planned to follow-up with cardiology for stress test.

Chief Complaint:
Follow up evaluation for patient with  HCC

Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( TRK gene: Negative; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Diagnosis*:
Treatment History:
Tremelimumab-actl + Durvalumab fb Durvalumab Q28D Cycle Length: 28 Number Cycles: 6 Start: C1D1 on 11/11/2024 Assoc Dx: Hepatocellular carcinoma LOT: 1st Line Metastatic or Recurrent 11/13/2024 C1 D1

	Tremelimumab-actl IV, G: 300 mg

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Pathology:
History of Present Illness:
68 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024.

Interval History:
since last seen in clinic, the patient received first immunotherapy infusion. he had an eventful 4 weeks with non specific symptoms that the patient and family attributes to panic attacks and anxiety. The pt had been complaining of severe abdominal pain throughout the last 4 weeks but kept denying it n prior attempts to obtain abdominal imaging and eventually presented to ED 12/6 when he had imaging that was reassuring. he has not been able to sleep and that resulted in significant fatigue. he dropped 4 pounds. denies fever, chills, nausea, vomiting. No diarrhea or constipation. he feels cold and tired.

Labs*:
Imaging:
Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.
{}
Problem List:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Abnormal iron profile (finding) ( ICD-10:R79.9 ;Abnormal finding of blood chemistry, unspecified )
	Abnormal iron profile (finding) ( ICD-10:R79.0 ;Abnormal level of blood mineral )
	Atypical chest pain ( ICD-10:I20.0 ;Unstable angina )
	Ear pain ( ICD-10:H92.02 ;Otalgia, left ear )
	Effects of immunotherapy ( ICD-10:Z51.12 ;Encounter for antineoplastic immunotherapy )
	Fatigue ( ICD-10:R53.83 ;Other fatigue )
	Fatigue ( ICD-10:R53.83 ;Other fatigue )
	Gallbladder calculus (disorder) ( ICD-10:K80.20 ;Calculus of gallbladder without cholecystitis without obstruction )
	Malabsorption ( ICD-10:K90.9 ;Intestinal malabsorption, unspecified )
	Thrombocytopenia ( ICD-10:D69.6 ;Thrombocytopenia, unspecified )
	Umbilical hernia (disorder) ( ICD-10:K42.9 ;Umbilical hernia without obstruction or gangrene )

Impression:
68-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC:

HCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hypervascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. He is currently status post left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score is 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. 

I previously counseled him about my concerns of peritoneal seeding with his rupture and my inclination of starting him on systemic therapy for HCC  based on the high likelihood of residual disease ( liver and peritoneum), low toxicity profile of therapy, and the encouraging results of EMERALD-1 trial suggesting adding systemic therapy to embolization could be of value among intermediate stage HCC patients.  Of note, liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. Commenced therapy on 11/11/2024.

Today, he is clinically ok with lab results within acceptable limits.  We will proceed with cycle 2 day 1 tremelimumab durvalumab.

Insomnia:
chronic. pt was on a medication previously that worked well. he is seeing the psychiatrist later today. likely to resume.

Fatigue:
Possibly related to insomnia. await sleep improvement before considering stimulants.

Neoplasm related pain:
Oxycodone script.

Plan:
Extensive counseling as above
Proceed with cycle 2 D1 of stride regimen.
Follow up with psyciatry.
RTC in 4 weeks with CBC, CMP, TFT and consideration of cycle 3.

Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

.

Imaging:
Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

	Thrombocytopenia ( ICD-10:D69.6 ;Thrombocytopenia, unspecified )
	Umbilical hernia (disorder) ( ICD-10:K42.9 ;Umbilical hernia without obstruction or gangrene )

Impression:
68-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC:

HCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hypervascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. He is currently status post left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score is 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. 

I previously counseled him about my concerns of peritoneal seeding with his rupture and my inclination of starting him on systemic therapy for HCC  based on the high likelihood of residual disease ( liver and peritoneum), low toxicity profile of therapy, and the encouraging results of EMERALD-1 trial suggesting adding systemic therapy to embolization could be of value among intermediate stage HCC patients.  Of note, liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. Commenced therapy on 11/11/2024.

Today, he is clinically ok with lab results within acceptable limits.  We will proceed with cycle 3 day 1 tremelimumab durvalumab.

Neoplasm related pain:
Oxycodone script.

Plan:
Extensive counseling as above
Proceed with cycle 3 D1 of stride regimen.
RTC in 4 weeks with CBC, CMP, TFT and consideration of cycle 4.

Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

Follow up evaluation for patient with  HCC

Tremelimumab-actl + Durvalumab fb Durvalumab Q28D Cycle Length: 28 Number Cycles: 6 Start: C1D1 on 11/11/2024 Assoc Dx: Hepatocellular carcinoma LOT: 1st Line Metastatic or Recurrent 11/13/2024 C1 D1

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Pathology:
History of Present Illness:
68 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024.

Interval History:
since last seen in clinic, the patient received 3rd immunotherapy infusion. He is doing much better. No anxiety.  No abdominal pain. He had mild nausea but no vomiting. denies fever, chills, nausea, vomiting. No diarrhea or constipation. he feels cold and tired but in general much better once his anxiety and sleep improved.

Hypertension

Imaging:
Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.

Problem List:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Abnormal iron profile (finding) ( ICD-10:R79.9 ;Abnormal finding of blood chemistry, unspecified )
	Abnormal iron profile (finding) ( ICD-10:R79.0 ;Abnormal level of blood mineral )
	Atypical chest pain ( ICD-10:I20.0 ;Unstable angina )
	Ear pain ( ICD-10:H92.02 ;Otalgia, left ear )
	Effects of immunotherapy ( ICD-10:Z51.12 ;Encounter for antineoplastic immunotherapy )
	Fatigue ( ICD-10:R53.83 ;Other fatigue )
	Fatigue ( ICD-10:R53.83 ;Other fatigue )
	Gallbladder calculus (disorder) ( ICD-10:K80.20 ;Calculus of gallbladder without cholecystitis without obstruction )
	Malabsorption ( ICD-10:K90.9 ;Intestinal malabsorption, unspecified )
	Thrombocytopenia ( ICD-10:D69.6 ;Thrombocytopenia, unspecified )
	Umbilical hernia (disorder) ( ICD-10:K42.9 ;Umbilical hernia without obstruction or gangrene )

Impression:
68-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC:

HCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hypervascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. He is currently status post left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score is 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. 

I previously counseled him about my concerns of peritoneal seeding with his rupture and my inclination of starting him on systemic therapy for HCC  based on the high likelihood of residual disease ( liver and peritoneum) and the low toxicity profile of therapy.  Of note, liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. Commenced therapy on 11/11/2024.

Today, he is clinically ok with lab results within acceptable limits.  We will proceed with cycle 5 day 1 of therapy.

Neoplasm related pain:
Oxycodone script.

Plan:
Extensive counseling as above
Proceed with cycle 5 D1 of stride regimen.
RTC in 4 weeks with CBC, CMP, TFT, CT CAP and consideration of cycle 6.

Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

.

Referring Provider:
Dr. Amar Gupta

Chief Complaint:
Follow up evaluation for patient with  HCC

Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( TRK gene: Negative; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Diagnosis*:
Treatment History:
Tremelimumab-actl + Durvalumab fb Durvalumab Q28D Cycle Length: 28 Number Cycles: 6 Start: C1D1 on 11/11/2024 Assoc Dx: Hepatocellular carcinoma LOT: 1st Line Metastatic or Recurrent 11/13/2024 C1 D1

	Tremelimumab-actl IV, G: 300 mg
	Imfinzi (Durvalumab IV), G: 1,500 mg

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Pathology:
History of Present Illness:
68 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024.

Interval History:
since last seen in clinic, the patient received 5th immunotherapy infusion. He is doing much better. No anxiety. Tolerating entecavir and Zoloft.   No abdominal pain. No nausea but no vomiting. denies fever, chills, nausea, vomiting. No diarrhea or constipation. He does report non specific intermittent periumbilical pain that is mild.

Labs*:
Imaging:
CT CAP 3/26/2025:
SUSPECTED RESIDUAL DISEASE in the margin of SEGMENT 8 fungating lesion.
soft tissue fullness at the root of mesentery.

Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.

Problem List:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Abnormal iron profile (finding) ( ICD-10:R79.9 ;Abnormal finding of blood chemistry, unspecified )
	Abnormal iron profile (finding) ( ICD-10:R79.0 ;Abnormal level of blood mineral )
	Atypical chest pain ( ICD-10:I20.0 ;Unstable angina )

	Effects of immunotherapy ( ICD-10:Z51.12 ;Encounter for antineoplastic immunotherapy )
	Fatigue ( ICD-10:R53.83 ;Other fatigue )
	Fatigue ( ICD-10:R53.83 ;Other fatigue )
	Gallbladder calculus (disorder) ( ICD-10:K80.20 ;Calculus of gallbladder without cholecystitis without obstruction )
	Malabsorption ( ICD-10:K90.9 ;Intestinal malabsorption, unspecified )
	Thrombocytopenia ( ICD-10:D69.6 ;Thrombocytopenia, unspecified )
	Umbilical hernia (disorder) ( ICD-10:K42.9 ;Umbilical hernia without obstruction or gangrene )

Impression:
68-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC:

HCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hypervascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. He is currently status post left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score is 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024.

Today, he is clinically ok with lab results within acceptable limits.  Review of CT scan showing no conclusive evidence of progression. We will proceed with cycle 6 day 1 of therapy.

Soft tissue infiltration around the mesenteric root:
Noted to worsen around March 2025. Unclear if related to disease, treatment or neither. will request EUS guided sampling. If not cancer related will discuss liver directed interventions to presumed residual disease in segment 8 treated lesion.

Neoplasm related pain:
Continue Oxycodone as needed.

Plan:
CT reviewed as above. Continue current therapy.
Proceed with cycle 6 D1 of stride regimen.
RTC in 4 weeks with CBC, CMP, TFT, and consideration of cycle 7.
Refer for consideration of EUS guided sampling of perivascular soft tissue infiltration.

Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Pathology:
History of Present Illness:
68 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024.

Interval History:
since last seen in clinic, the patient received 6th immunotherapy infusion. He is doing much better. No anxiety. Tolerating entecavir and Zoloft.   No abdominal pain. No nausea but no vomiting. denies fever, chills, nausea, vomiting. No diarrhea or constipation. He does report non specific intermittent periumbilical pain that is mild.

BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024.

Interval History:
since last seen in clinic, the patient received 7th immunotherapy infusion. He is doing much better. No anxiety. Tolerating entecavir and Zoloft.   No abdominal pain. No nausea but no vomiting. denies fever, chills, nausea, vomiting. No diarrhea or constipation. He does report non specific intermittent periumbilical pain that is mild.

SUSPECTED RESIDUAL DISEASE in the margin of SEGMENT 8 fungating lesion.
soft tissue fullness at the root of mesentery.

Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.

Impression:
68-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC:

HCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hypervascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. He is currently status post left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score is 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024.

Today, he is clinically ok with lab results within acceptable limits.  Review of CT scan showing improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Unclear if this is related to his HCC as the location and behavior is inconsistent. DDX is wide and include either a second malignancy, atypical HCC behavior, Immune therapy related lymph proliferative process, Immune therapy driven retroperitoneal fibrosis , etc. for that will Proceed with another cycle of immunotherapy while we refer pt for laparoscopic sampling of that area.

Soft tissue infiltration around the mesenteric root:
Noted to worsen around March 2025. Unclear if related to disease, treatment or neither. Discussed with GI EUS guided sampling but technically challenging. worsened in May 2025. Discussed with Dr Gupta and will plan on surgical sampling.

Neoplasm related pain:
Continue Oxycodone as needed.

Plan:
CT reviewed as above.
Proceed with cycle 8 D1 of stride regimen.
Refer to Hepatobiliary surgery for sampling as above.
RTC in 4 weeks with CBC, CMP and consideration for cycle 9.

Laith Abushahin MD

Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( Mismatch Repair (MMR) status: Proficient; Microsatellite instability (MSI) status: Low; TRK gene: Negative; RET fusion status: Unknown; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) Q30D (RCC, HCC, Differentiated Thyroid, Uterine, Neuroendocrine Tumor) Cycle Length: 30 Number Cycles: 6 Start: C1D1 on 06/25/2025 Assoc Dx: Hepatocellular carcinoma LOT: 2nd Line Metastatic 06/25/2025 C1 D1

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.

Pathology:

History of Present Illness:
68 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024.

Interval History:
Since last seen in clinic, the patient underwent robotic laparoscopic sampling of aortocaval node on 6/11/2025. He tolerated procedure ok. intra operatively was found to have no peritoneal disease. the node was malignant appearing and only an incisional biopsy was done showing poorly differentiated malignancy. several stains were negative with the exception of AFP and Cytokeratin likely reflecting poorly differentiated HCC. No abdominal pain. No nausea but no vomiting. denies fever, chills, nausea, vomiting. No diarrhea or constipation. He does report non specific intermittent periumbilical pain that is mild.

Impression:
68-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC:

HCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. He is currently status post left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score is 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. Review of CT scan showing improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Unclear if this is related to his HCC as the location and behavior is inconsistent. DDX is wide and include either a second malignancy, atypical HCC behavior, Immune therapy related lymph proliferative process, Immune therapy driven retroperitoneal fibrosis. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming HCC. 

Today, he is clinically ok with lab results within acceptable limits. discussed progression through first line. will proceed with second line therapy with Cabozantinib. Discussed mechanism of action as a multikinase inhibitor and its role in disease control. Reviewed dosing instructions, emphasizing administration on an empty stomach and adherence to prescribed schedule. Patient was informed of common adverse effects including diarrhea, fatigue, hypertension, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, and potential hepatotoxicity. Advised to report any new or worsening symptoms promptly. Plan includes regular monitoring of blood pressure, liver function tests, and complete blood counts at baseline and periodically throughout treatment. Dose adjustments will be considered based on toxicity profile and tolerability. Patient verbalized understanding and agreed to follow up for ongoing assessment of efficacy and side effects. I sent a script of 60 mg po daily and gave him a sample of 40 mg po ( 15 tablets). Will return in 1 one week to see one of my colleagues with labs to assess tolerance. If he is tolerating that well he can proceed with the 60 mg script. Otherwise a new script for a lower dose can be given

Plan:
Path reviewed as above
Discontinue ICI
Proceed with 2nd line Cabozantinib.
Script sent. sample given as above
Return in 1 week for toxicity assessment 
RTC t myself on 7/17.

Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

.

No abdominal pain. No nausea but no vomiting. denies fever, chills, nausea, vomiting. No diarrhea or constipation. He does report non specific intermittent periumbilical pain that is mild.

Dr. Amar Gupta

Chief Complaint:

Principal Diagnosis: Hepatocellular carcinoma ( Mismatch Repair (MMR) status: Proficient; Microsatellite instability (MSI) status: Low; TRK gene: Negative; RET fusion status: Unknown;  ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Treatment History*: 
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.

Pathology: 

History of Present Illness: 
68 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024.

Interval History: 
68-year-old male with multiple concerns returns for continued management and treatment of his hepatocellular carcinoma.  He started cabozantinib at a 40 mg dosing and is tolerating it rather well with no major adverse effects reported.  He does have concern over his weight which has trended down significantly.  He said 1 barrier is that he has to fast for both his hepatitis medicine as well as the cabozantinib and he will check with pharmacy today to see if he could take the cabozantinib at the same time as his hepatitis B medication to open up more hours in the day for nutrition.  He is also having issues affording the medication and tried to fill the 60 mg Rx today in our pharmacy.  He was requesting additional samples as he only has 5 pills left but there were no samples for me to provide to the patient.  I sent a message out regarding options and asked that he call back on Monday to check status.  We also discussed supplement drinks to help boost his calorie intake/protein support.  No recent fevers.  He has nonspecific intermittent periumbilical pain which is mild.  Otherwise no abdominal pain, no nausea, no vomiting.  He did not experience diarrhea until yesterday when he had 4 episodes.  It is possible that when he goes up to 60 mg dosing he will have more loose bowel movements.  We discussed the use of Imodium.  We also reviewed the other samples he was given for nausea and for diarrhea.  No recent fevers or chills.  No other complaints.

68 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024.

Interval History: 
68-year-old male with multiple concerns returns for continued management and treatment of his hepatocellular carcinoma.  He started cabozantinib at a 40 mg dosing and had tolerated it rather well with no major adverse effects reported.He has put 2 lbs on which he is very happy about being that weight loss was a major concern at the prior visit.  He is still hesitant about the sugar in the Boost drinks but given his glucose average on recent labs (non-fasting) we discussed that sugar is not currently a concern and that it is more important he improves his protein nutrition.  Alternately, we discussed there are other lower-sugar supplement drinks such as Glycerna available but would suggest that more for patients with uncontrolled diabetes.  He has pain after eating and we discussed strategies to use pain meds a little more frequently in order to be comfortable enough to get his calories in.  He has not needed to use medications for nausea nor diarrhea thus far but has them at home if needed.  If his is constipated, we talked about using something more gentle than suppositories such as MiraLAX for more consistent soft BM as a suggestion.He is here with his son today and the issue with filling RX/having it covered by insurance for the cabo 60 mg dosing was resolved and he will have medication for one year at least without an out-of-pocket cost.  No recent fevers, chills or other complaints outside of post-prandial abdominal pain.

Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.

Impression/ Plan:  68-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC:HCC:As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. He is currently status post left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score is 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. Review of CT scan showing improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Unclear if this is related to his HCC as the location and behavior is inconsistent. DDX is wide and include either a second malignancy, atypical HCC behavior, Immune therapy related lymph proliferative process, Immune therapy driven retroperitoneal fibrosis. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming HCC. Today, 7/17/2025:ââNeoplasm related pain is stableââCabozantinib was being well-tolerated at 40 mg dosing; he has since resolved insurance issues and was able to fill 60 mg dosing.  He is also tolerating this dose well. CMP is still pending; he has mild neutropenia/thrombocytopenia that does not require any dose adjustments.  We will see him back for MD visit/labs in about 2 weeks but if any issues arise in the meantime, he is welcome to return prn before next scheduled visit.  ââneiplasm-related pain: pain is well controlled for the most part but we discussed strategies to get around post prandial pain such as taking pain meds prior to eating.  âânutrition:  weight is up 2 lbs; we discussed that Boost is fine to take and the glucose in the supplement is not likely to adversely effect prognosis/cancer growth given his stable non-fasting glucose trending.  We discussed the importance of nutritional supplements to improve his overall nutritional status/gain back some weight.  Glycerna is an option if he continues to be uncomfortable with the amount of glucose in Boost but I don't think Glycerna is really needed for this patient. ââHe is doing overall on well 60 mg dosing and I will put a 2 week MD visit in with labs for his next visit.  30 minutes spent face-to-face with the patient reviewing many of his questions and concerns.

.

 Erica Carone PA

Chandana Reddy, D.O.

History of Present Illness:
68 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC.

Interval History:
Since last seen in clinic, the patient had been on Cabozantinib , initially at 40 mg po 6/25 through 7/10 and then at 60 mg daily. He reports diarrhea, nausea, low appetite and some wt loss. BP readings had been ok. No bleeding or hemorrhage. continues to complain of abdominal pain. takes tramadol. denies fever, chills, vomiting.

Worsening retroperitoneal soft tissue infiltration around the celiac.

CT CAP 3/26/2025:
SUSPECTED RESIDUAL DISEASE in the margin of SEGMENT 8 fungating lesion.
soft tissue fullness at the root of mesentery.

Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.

Problem List:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Abnormal iron profile (finding) ( ICD-10:R79.0 ;Abnormal level of blood mineral )
	Abnormal iron profile (finding) ( ICD-10:R79.9 ;Abnormal finding of blood chemistry, unspecified )
	Atypical chest pain ( ICD-10:I20.0 ;Unstable angina )
	Ear pain ( ICD-10:H92.02 ;Otalgia, left ear )
	Effects of immunotherapy ( ICD-10:Z51.12 ;Encounter for antineoplastic immunotherapy )
	Fatigue ( ICD-10:R53.83 ;Other fatigue )
	Fatigue ( ICD-10:R53.83 ;Other fatigue )
	Gallbladder calculus (disorder) ( ICD-10:K80.20 ;Calculus of gallbladder without cholecystitis without obstruction )
	Malabsorption ( ICD-10:K90.9 ;Intestinal malabsorption, unspecified )
	Mass of retroperitoneal structure (finding) ( ICD-10:C48.0 ;Malignant neoplasm of retroperitoneum )
	Neoplasm related pain ( ICD-10:G89.3 ;Neoplasm related pain (acute) (chronic) )
	Thrombocytopenia ( ICD-10:D69.6 ;Thrombocytopenia, unspecified )
	Umbilical hernia (disorder) ( ICD-10:K42.9 ;Umbilical hernia without obstruction or gangrene )

Impression:
68-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/ iCC:

HCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score is 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where  AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 6/25/2025. Of note, his molecular profiling showed IDH-1 , TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of at least mixed type chlangio/ HCC.

Today, he is clinically ok with lab results within acceptable limits. He appears to have significant diarrhea and progressive thrombocytopenia since increasing the dose. will adjust to 40 mg po daily and recheck in 2 weeks. 

Upon progression could consider therapy directed to cholangiocarcinoma as above

Abdominal Pain:
Continue Tramadol
consider celiac plexus block.

Plan:
Molecular profiling reviewed as above
Continue with 2nd line Cabozantinib. 
Dose reduce to 40 mg po daily
Return in 2 week for toxicity assessment, weight management, follow up on PLT, bilirubin and decision regarding celiac block.
Plan for imaging in August. Subsequent lines could take cholangiocarcinoma approach as above.

Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

Chief Complaint:
Follow up evaluation for patient with  HCC

Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( Mismatch Repair (MMR) status: Proficient; Microsatellite instability (MSI) status: Low; TRK gene: Negative; RET fusion status: Unknown; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Diagnosis*:
Treatment History:
Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) Q30D (RCC, HCC, Differentiated Thyroid, Uterine, Neuroendocrine Tumor) Cycle Length: 30 Number Cycles: 6 Start: C1D1 on 06/25/2025 Assoc Dx: Hepatocellular carcinoma LOT: 2nd Line Metastatic 06/25/2025 C1 D1

	Cabometyx (Cabozantinib Oral (Cabometyx)), 60 mg qday

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.

Pathology:
A. Aortocaval lymph node, biopsy: Positive for metastatic carcinoma. The morphological and staining findings are compatible with metastatic moderately to poorly differentiated HCC. 

History of Present Illness:
68 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC.

Interval History:
Since last seen in clinic, the patient had been on Cabozantinib  40 mg with better tolerance. Diarrhea persists but its improved and manageable. No nausea, better appetite and didnt lose further wt. BP readings had been ok. No bleeding or hemorrhage. continues to complain of abdominal pain. takes tramadol. denies fever, chills, vomiting. 

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hepatitis C
BPH
Past Surgical History:

The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC.

Interval History:
Since last seen in clinic, the patient had been on Cabozantinib  40 mg with better tolerance. Diarrhea persists but its improved and manageable. No nausea, better appetite and didn't lose further wt. BP readings had been ok. No bleeding or hemorrhage. continues to complain of abdominal pain. takes tramadol. denies fever, chills, vomiting. On careful questioning, the patient describes the pain as improving over the last 2 months from a peak point of pain around the sampling of his disease in June.

Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.

Impression:
68-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/ iCC:

HCC vs mixed HCC/ iCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB.  Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 6/25/2025. Of note, his molecular profiling showed IDH-1 , TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/ HCC vs clear cell subtype HCC.

Today, he is clinically ok with lab results within acceptable limits. Review of CT scan show clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what is unclear is if it represent true progression through cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appears to support the latter. For that will Continue Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitor carefully clinically and radiographically. requested daily symptom journal that we will check q 2 weeks and plan to repeat the scan in 6-8 weeks. If progression confirmed I will attempt to seek Ivosidenib therapy targeting mixed HCC/ iCC.

Abdominal Pain:
Continue Tramadol
consider celiac plexus block.

Plan:
Extensive counseling as above for a total of 60 minutes.
CT reviewed as above.
Continue with 2nd line Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily
Daily pain journal.
RTC in 2 weeks to review
Upon confirming progression will seek IDH targeting.
.

Laith Abushahin MD

Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

Labs*:
Imaging:
 CT CAP 8/26/2025:
Progression of soft tissue around the IVC/ aorta

CT CAP 5/20/2025:
Improving residual disease in liver cavity
Worsening retroperitoneal soft tissue infiltration around the celiac.

CT CAP 3/26/2025:
SUSPECTED RESIDUAL DISEASE in the margin of SEGMENT 8 fungating lesion.
soft tissue fullness at the root of mesentery.

Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.
