Impression:
69-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/ iCC:

HCC vs mixed HCC/ iCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB.  Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 6/25/2025. Of note, his molecular profiling showed IDH-1 , TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/ HCC vs clear cell subtype HCC. Review of CT scan late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. 

Today, he is clinically showing signs of progression. Counseled extensively regarding options. as he had progression through immunotherapy and cabozantinib I will plan on Ivosidenib therapy targeting mixed HCC/ iCC.

Abdominal Pain:
Continue Tramadol
consider celiac plexus block.

Plan:
Extensive counseling as above for a total of 60 minutes.
Plan to discontinue 2nd line Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily
Plan for Ivosedinib as above.
RTC in 2 weeks to review
CT CAP around the time to start therapy for baseline.

Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

Referring Provider:
Dr. Amar Gupta

Chief Complaint:
Follow up evaluation for patient with HCC/ mixed HCC iCCA

Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( Mismatch Repair (MMR) status: Proficient; Microsatellite instability (MSI) status: Low; TRK gene: Negative; RET fusion status: Unknown; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Diagnosis*:
Treatment History:
Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) Q30D (RCC, HCC, Differentiated Thyroid, Uterine, Neuroendocrine Tumor) Cycle Length: 30 Number Cycles: 6 Start: C1D1 on 06/25/2025 Assoc Dx: Hepatocellular carcinoma LOT: 2nd Line Metastatic 06/25/2025 C1 D1

	Cabometyx (Cabozantinib Oral (Cabometyx)), 60 mg qday

Ivosidenib Q30D Cycle Length: 30 Number Cycles: 6 Start: C1D1 on 09/22/2025 Assoc Dx: Primary carcinoma liver and/or biliary system (disorder) LOT: 3rd Line Metastatic 09/22/2025 C1 D1

	Ivosidenib Oral, 500 mg qday

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Durvalumab Trememilumab through May 2025
Cabozantinib 6/25-9/17/2025
Ivosednib 9/17

Pathology:
A. Aortocaval lymph node, biopsy: Positive for metastatic carcinoma. The morphological and staining findings are compatible with metastatic moderately to poorly differentiated HCC. 
   
History of Present Illness:
69 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC. Eventually with clinical and radiographic progression, switched to Ivosednib due to IDH-1 mutation 9/17/2025.

Interval History:
The patient has discontinued Cabozantinib prior to today's visit and reports worsening clinical condition since last seen. He describes progressive weight loss, currently weighing 122 lbs. His pain has significantly worsened over the last several weeks, particularly back pain extending from neck to lower spine with pain scores ranging from 4-7. He experienced nausea and vomiting on September 15th, with subsequent spinal cord pain after vomiting episodes. His appetite remains poor and he is eating less than usual, primarily consuming small portions of oatmeal with ground flax, almond milk, avocado and banana for both breakfast and lunch. He reports sour taste in mouth. Diarrhea continues at 4-5 times daily, unchanged from prior. He denies fevers, chills, blood in stool, shortness of breath, chest pain, or palpable masses. He takes tramadol only daily before sleep due to concerns about work-related drowsiness, living with pain during the day. He was surprised to receive approval for the new medication Ivosidenib today.

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hepatitis C
BPH   
   
Medications:

	Ivosidenib Oral 250 mg tablet 500 mg orally daily. Take with or without food. Avoid taking with a high-fat meal.
	Lisinopril Oral 40 mg tablet po daily
	Lorazepam Oral 0.5 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every day at bedtime as needed for sleep.
	Tramadol Oral 50 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every 6 hours.
	Tamsulosin Oral 0.4 mg capsule 1 capsule PO daily
	Entecavir Oral 0.5 mg tablet q d
	Omeprazole Oral Delayed Release Capsule 20 mg capsule,delayed release(DR/EC) po daily

Medications reviewed and reconciled with patient.

No known medication allergies

Smoking Status:
Smoking Tobacco : Never smoker; Smokeless Tobacco : none found; Vaping : none found

Social History:
Patient from Ethiopia originally.  Has 1 son and 1 daughter.  Never smoker but currently vaping.

Impression:
69-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/ iCC:

HCC vs mixed HCC/ iCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC.  Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass.  His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB.  Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 6/25/2025. Of note, his molecular profiling showed IDH-1 , TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/ HCC vs clear cell subtype HCC. Review of CT scan late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression. Switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosednib targeting mixed HCC/ iCC. Commenced 9/17/2025.

Today, he is clinically showing signs of progression. Counseled extensively regarding Ivosednib and anticipated toxicities. ECG performed with QTc ~ 400. Counseled regarding drug interaction , administration instructions and toxicities. Will obtain CT imaging to assess the reason for the sudden worsening of pain.

Abdominal Pain:
Continue Tramadol
consider celiac plexus block.

Encounter of Antineoplastic agents:
Lorazepam for antiemetics as needed to avoid QTc prolongation overlap.

Plan:
Discontinued second line Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily due to clinical progression
Starting Ivosidenib 500 mg daily (two 250 mg tablets) taken daily
Counseled patient to avoid high fat meals when taking Ivosidenib to prevent increased absorption
Ordered CT abdomen and pelvis today to assess disease status prior to starting new therapy
EKG to be obtained to assess QTc
Continue current tramadol regimen for pain management
Lorazepam script
Nutritional counseling provided regarding small frequent meals and avoiding high fiber foods until diarrhea improves
RTC in 1 week with labs and repeat ECG
   
Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

extracted.patient_update: The patient has discontinued Cabozantinib prior to today's visit and reports worsening clinical condition since last seen. He describes progressive weight loss, currently weighing 122 lbs. His pain has significantly worsened over the last several weeks, particularly back pain extending from neck to lower spine with pain scores ranging from 4-7. He experienced nausea and vomiting on September 15th, with subsequent spinal cord pain after vomiting episodes. His appetite remains poor and he is eating less than usual, primarily consuming small portions of oatmeal with ground flax, almond milk, avocado and banana for both breakfast and lunch. He reports sour taste in mouth. Diarrhea continues at 4-5 times daily, unchanged from prior. He denies fevers, chills, blood in stool, shortness of breath, chest pain, or palpable masses. He takes tramadol only daily before sleep due to concerns about work-related drowsiness, living with pain during the day. He was surprised to receive approval for the new medication Ivosidenib today.

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Durvalumab Trememilumab through May 2025

Ivosednib 9/17

Pathology:
A. Aortocaval lymph node, biopsy: Positive for metastatic carcinoma. The morphological and staining findings are compatible with metastatic moderately to poorly differentiated HCC. 
   
   
History of Present Illness:
69 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC. Eventually with clinical and radiographic progression, switched to Ivosednib due to IDH-1 mutation 9/17/2025.

Interval History:
Patient reports 12 days into Ivosidenib therapy with persistent abdominal and back pain at same intensity (7/10). Diarrhea has improved from previous 5-7 episodes daily to approximately twice daily with some formed stools noted for the first time in recent days, though still experiencing mixed solid and liquid stools particularly at night. Continued weight loss of 1 pound since last visit with stable appetite. No blood in stool, no fever, chills, or lower extremity edema. Patient expressing concern about medication timing and food interactions, specifically regarding high-fat meal restrictions and appropriate dosing schedule.

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hepatitis C
BPH

Review of CT scan late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression. Switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosednib targeting mixed HCC/ iCC. Commenced 9/17/2025.

Today,  On 09/29/2025, patient is 12 days into Ivosidenib therapy with stable abdominal and back pain but improved diarrhea. Recent imaging shows slight disease progression with 10% increase in retroperitoneal mass size and new small nodules concerning for peritoneal implants. Portal vein compression continues. Liver function tests have normalized. Continue current treatment approach with close monitoring. 

Abdominal Pain:
Continue Tramadol, Add oxycodone and bowel regimen
consider celiac plexus block.

Encounter of Antineoplastic agents:
Lorazepam for antiemetics as needed to avoid QTc prolongation overlap.

Plan:
Continue Ivosidenib 500 mg daily with counseling reinforced regarding avoiding high fat meals to prevent increased absorption and proper administration timing
EKG to be obtained to assess QTc interval
Continue current tramadol regimen for pain management and add oxycodone
Lorazepam available as needed for antiemetics to avoid QTc prolongation overlap
Nutritional counseling reinforced regarding small frequent meals
Return to clinic in 2 weeks with labs
Repeat imaging planned in approximately 4 weeks (end of October) to assess treatment response
Close monitoring for signs of disease progression and treatment toxicities

Laith Abushahin MD

Chandana Reddy, D.O.

Dr. Amar Gupta

Chief Complaint:
Follow-up evaluation for patient with HCC/mixed HCC-CCA on  Ivosidenib therapy

Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( Mismatch Repair (MMR) status: Proficient; Microsatellite instability (MSI) status: Low; TRK gene: Negative; RET fusion status: Unknown; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Diagnosis*:
Treatment History:
Ivosidenib Q30D Cycle Length: 30 Number Cycles: 6 Start: C1D1 on 09/18/2025 Assoc Dx: Primary carcinoma liver and/or biliary system (disorder) LOT: 3rd Line Metastatic 09/22/2025 C1 D1

	Ivosidenib Oral, 500 mg qday

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Durvalumab Trememilumab through May 2025
Cabozantinib 6/25-9/17/2025
Ivosednib 9/17

Pathology:
A. Aortocaval lymph node, biopsy: Positive for metastatic carcinoma. The morphological and staining findings are compatible with metastatic moderately to poorly differentiated HCC. 
   
 
   
History of Present Illness:
69 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC. Eventually with clinical and radiographic progression, switched to Ivosednib due to IDH-1 mutation 9/17/2025.

Interval History:
Patient reports approximately 4 weeks into Ivosidenib therapy with improved pain management patterns, taking oxycodone approximately half the days over the past 2 weeks compared to nearly daily use in prior weeks. He continues to experience persistent abdominal and back pain but notes improved tolerance. Diarrhea has significantly improved and resolved, with bowel movements normalizing to approximately 3 times daily after weeks of frequent loose stools. Patient reports continued weight loss of 1 pound since last visit with maintained good appetite and ability to eat without restriction. He denies swallowing difficulties, nausea, vomiting, shortness of breath, cough, lower extremity edema, abdominal distension, mouth sores, or skin changes. Patient expresses concern about disease progression and effectiveness of current therapy.

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hepatitis C
BPH

	Tramadol Oral 50 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every 6 hours.
	Entecavir Oral 0.5 mg tablet q d

Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.
   
CT abdomen and pelvis 09/17/2025: Progression of retroperitoneal mass from 5.5 cm to 6.2 cm (approximately 10% increase). New small nodules noted in abdomen measuring 4-8 millimeters, concerning for possible peritoneal implants. Portal vein compression persists.

Impression:
69-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/iCC:

HCC vs mixed HCC/iCC: 
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC. Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass. His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 06/25/2025. Of note, his molecular profiling showed IDH-1, TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/HCC vs clear cell subtype HCC. Review of CT scan late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression. Switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosidenib targeting mixed HCC/iCC. Commenced 09/17/2025. On 10/14/2025, patient continues on Ivosidenib therapy with improved symptom management including resolution of diarrhea and decreased frequency of pain medication use. Patient demonstrates continued weight loss but maintains good appetite and functional status. Disease monitoring scan planned for end of October  Patient counseled regarding potential treatment options if current therapy proves ineffective.

Abdominal Pain:
Improved pain medication usage pattern with oxycodone taken approximately half the days over past 2 weeks. 

Plan:
Continue Ivosidenib 500 mg daily with reinforced counseling regarding avoiding high fat meals to prevent increased absorption and proper administration timing
Provide oxycodone prescription refill for pain management as needed
Schedule imaging study for end of October 2025 to assess treatment response after approximately 6 weeks of therapy
Return to clinic upon completion of imaging study with same-day or next available appointment
Close monitoring for signs of disease progression and treatment toxicities
Counseled patient regarding potential future treatment options including chemotherapy or radiation therapy if current therapy proves ineffective
Procedure:

Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

69 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC. Eventually with clinical and radiographic progression, switched to Ivosednib due to IDH-1 mutation 9/17/2025.

Interval History:
Patient reports approximately 6 weeks on Ivosidenib therapy with persistent abdominal and back pain managed primarily with oxycodone at nighttime due to work constraints. He describes the pain as non-stop but continues working during the day. New onset watery diarrhea started a few days ago with up to 4 bowel movements daily, which is a change from his previously resolved diarrhea noted at last visit. Weight remains stable at 120.4 pounds with maintained appetite and ability to eat without restriction. Patient underwent CT imaging today to assess treatment response. He expresses cautious optimism about treatment effectiveness based on imaging appearance.

BPH

3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.

Impression:
69-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/iCC:

HCC vs mixed HCC/iCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC. Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass. His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 06/25/2025. Of note, his molecular profiling showed IDH-1, TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/HCC vs clear cell subtype HCC. Review of CT scan late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression. Switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosidenib targeting mixed HCC/iCC. Commenced 09/17/2025. On 10/29/2025, imaging demonstrates first evidence of disease stability with retroperitoneal mass measuring 6 x 3.5 cm compared to 6.2 x 3.7 cm on 09/17/2025, representing stabilization after multiple prior scans showing progression. Venous compression appears improved with less narrowing, suggesting decreased mass effect. Patient continues on Ivosidenib therapy with persistent pain managed with oxycodone and new onset watery diarrhea requiring evaluation for treatment-related toxicity vs other etiology.

Abdominal Pain:
Persistent pain managed primarily with nighttime oxycodone due to work constraints. Pain remains non-stop but functionally managed.

Diarrhea:
New onset watery diarrhea with up to 4 bowel movements daily, contrasting with previously resolved diarrhea. Consideration for pancreatic enzyme supplementation given potential malabsorption.

Plan:
Continue Ivosidenib 500 mg daily with reinforced counseling regarding avoiding high fat meals to prevent increased absorption and proper administration timing
Consider pancreatic enzyme supplementation for new onset diarrhea and potential malabsorption
Provide diarrhea medication for symptom management
Close monitoring for signs of disease progression and treatment toxicities
Return to clinic in 3 weeks for continued assessment of treatment response and symptom management
Counseled patient regarding encouraging imaging results showing first evidence of disease stability and importance of continuing current therapy
Procedure:

Laith Abushahin MD

Chandana Reddy, D.O.

Abdominal MRI 10/18/2024:
1.  Stable appearance of 7.6 cm mass in segment IVb of the liver, compatible with treated hepatoma. Persistent peripheral enhancement along superior anterior margin is not frankly nodular but is suspicious for a residual viable tumor, LR TR equivocal. Recommend 2 month follow-up MRI. 
 
2.  No evidence of persistent active extravasation.

3. Subtle cirrhotic liver morphology. No hepatic steatosis or iron deposition

Abdomen CTA 10/17/2024:

1.  Interval embolization of the hepatoma with persistent wispy enhancement within the anterior superior margin of the tumor, indeterminate for tumoral enhancement versus continued hemorrhage/extravasation.
2.  Suspected interval rupture of the hepatoma inferiorly with disruption of the pseudocapsule and mild increased surrounding hematoma. Repeat MRI may be helpful for more direct comparison to post embolization MRI.
3.  Stable hemoperitoneum in the pelvis.
   
CT abdomen and pelvis 09/17/2025: Progression of retroperitoneal mass from 5.5 cm to 6.2 cm (approximately 10% increase). New small nodules noted in abdomen measuring 4-8 millimeters, concerning for possible peritoneal implants. Portal vein compression persists.
   
- CT abdomen/pelvis 10/29/2025: Retroperitoneal mass measuring 6 x 3.5 cm compared to previous measurement of 6.2 x 3.7 cm on 09/17/2025, representing first evidence of disease stability after multiple prior scans showing progression. Vena cava compression appears improved with less narrowing noted.

Impression:
69-year-old male patient with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/iCC:

HCC vs mixed HCC/iCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC. Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass. His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 06/25/2025. Of note, his molecular profiling showed IDH-1, TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/HCC vs clear cell subtype HCC. Review of CT scan late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression. Switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosidenib targeting mixed HCC/iCC. Commenced 09/17/2025. On 10/29/2025, imaging demonstrates first evidence of disease stability with retroperitoneal mass measuring 6 x 3.5 cm compared to 6.2 x 3.7 cm on 09/17/2025, representing stabilization after multiple prior scans showing progression. Venous compression appears improved with less narrowing, suggesting decreased mass effect. 11/24/2025 Patient continues on Ivosidenib therapy with significant clinical improvement including resolution of pain (off oxycodone for 10 days) and weight stabilization with 1-pound gain to 121 lbs. Recent laboratory studies show normal kidney function, adequate protein levels, excellent liver function, and improved blood counts, supporting continued treatment response.

Abdominal Pain:
11/24/2025 Marked improvement in persistent pain with complete discontinuation of oxycodone 10 days ago and sustained pain relief, representing significant clinical improvement from prior non-stop pain requiring nighttime oxycodone.

Diarrhea:
11/24/2025 Substantial improvement in bowel function with reduction from previous watery diarrhea of up to 4 bowel movements daily to approximately 2 bowel movements of variable consistency without blood, suggesting resolution of treatment-related gastrointestinal toxicity.

Plan:
Continue Ivosidenib 500 mg daily with reinforced counseling regarding avoiding high fat meals to prevent increased absorption and proper administration timing
Close monitoring for signs of disease progression and treatment toxicities given excellent clinical response
Obtain CT scan in 3 weeks for continued assessment of treatment response
Counseled patient regarding encouraging clinical improvement showing significant symptom resolution and importance of continuing current therapy
Procedure:
   

Laith Abushahin MD

Chandana Reddy, D.O.

PATIENT: GUANGUL ZEKIROS

MRN: 1394784

Date of Service: 12/30/2025

Referring Provider:
Dr. Amar Gupta

Chief Complaint:
Follow-up evaluation for patient with HCC/mixed HCC-CCA on Ivosidenib therapy with recent imaging

Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( Mismatch Repair (MMR) status: Proficient; Microsatellite instability (MSI) status: Low; TRK gene: Negative; RET fusion status: Unknown; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Treatment History:
Ivosidenib Q30D Cycle Length: 30 Number Cycles: 6 Start: C1D1 on 09/18/2025 Assoc Dx: Primary carcinoma liver and/or biliary system (disorder) LOT: 3rd Line Metastatic 09/22/2025 C1 D1

	Ivosidenib Oral, 500 mg qday

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Durvalumab Trememilumab through May 2025
Cabozantinib 6/25-9/17/2025
Ivosednib 9/17

Pathology:
A. Aortocaval lymph node, biopsy: Positive for metastatic carcinoma. The morphological and staining findings are compatible with metastatic moderately to poorly differentiated HCC. 
    
   
History of Present Illness:
69 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC. Eventually with clinical and radiographic progression, switched to Ivosednib due to IDH-1 mutation 9/17/2025.

Interval History:
Patient reports marked clinical improvement since last visit with complete resolution of abdominal pain, having discontinued oxycodone entirely 10 days ago and remaining pain-free. Weight has increased from 121 to 124.6 lbs, representing continued weight gain and improved nutritional status. Appetite remains good with adequate oral intake and no swallowing difficulties. Bowel function continues to improve with diarrhea episodes less frequent, occurring fewer than 3 times daily without the prolonged bathroom time previously experienced. Patient continues working increased hours. He denies fever, chills, shortness of breath, or lower extremity edema. Patient had hepatology visit yesterday with reassuring liver function discussion.

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hepatitis C
BPH

	Tramadol Oral 50 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every 6 hours.
	Ivosidenib Oral 250 mg tablet 500 mg orally daily. Take with or without food. Avoid taking with a high-fat meal.

New lymph nodes identified in retroperitoneal region. New implant noted between kidney and lower liver. Multiple areas showing progression both inside and outside of liver.

Impression:
Guangul Zekiros is a 69-year-old male with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/iCC.

HCC vs mixed HCC/iCC:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC. Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass. His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 06/25/2025. Of note, his molecular profiling showed IDH-1, TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/HCC vs clear cell subtype HCC. Review of CT late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression. Switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosidenib targeting mixed HCC/iCC. Commenced 09/17/2025. On 10/29/2025, imaging demonstrates first evidence of disease stability with retroperitoneal mass measuring 6 x 3.5 cm compared to 6.2 x 3.7 cm on 09/17/2025, representing stabilization after multiple prior scans showing progression. Venous compression appears improved with less narrowing, suggesting decreased mass effect. 11/24/2025 Patient continues on Ivosidenib therapy with significant clinical improvement including resolution of pain and weight stabilization with 1-pound gain to 121 lbs. Recent laboratory studies show normal kidney function, adequate protein levels, excellent liver function, and improved blood counts, supporting continued treatment response. 12/30/2025 Patient demonstrates paradoxical clinical course with continued symptomatic improvement including sustained pain relief off oxycodone for 10 days and further weight gain to 124.6 lbs, however imaging reveals clear disease progression with retroperitoneal mass enlargement to 7 x 3.85 cm and new areas of disease within liver and additional lymph nodes, indicating treatment failure with Ivosidenib requiring immediate treatment modification.

Plan:
Discontinue Ivosidenib due to clear radiographic progression despite clinical improvement
Plan to develop new treatment regimen over the next week focusing on combination approach to address both hepatocellular carcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma components
Considering chemotherapy plus immunotherapy combination regimen to provide broad coverage for mixed tumor characteristics
   

Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

.

Weight has increased from 121 to 124.6 lbs, representing continued weight gain and improved nutritional status. Appetite remains good with adequate oral intake and no swallowing difficulties. Bowel function continues to improve with diarrhea episodes less frequent, occurring fewer than 3 times daily without the prolonged bathroom time previously experienced. Patient continues working increased hours. He denies fever, chills, shortness of breath, or lower extremity edema. Patient had hepatology visit yesterday with reassuring liver function discussion.

Follow-up evaluation for mixed hepatocellular carcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma with treatment planning

Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( Mismatch Repair (MMR) status: Proficient; Microsatellite instability (MSI) status: Low; TRK gene: Negative; RET fusion status: Unknown; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Treatment History:
Ivosidenib Q30D Cycle Length: 30 Number Cycles: 6 Start: C1D1 on 09/18/2025 Assoc Dx: Primary carcinoma liver and/or biliary system (disorder) LOT: 3rd Line Metastatic 09/22/2025 C1 D1

	Ivosidenib Oral, 500 mg qday

Durvalumab + Gemcitabine D1,8 + Cisplatin D1,8 Q21D Cycle Length: 21 Number Cycles: 8 Start: C1D1 on 01/12/2026 Assoc Dx: Primary carcinoma liver and/or biliary system (disorder) LOT: Other Treatment Intent: Palliative01/12/2026 C6 D1

	Imfinzi (Durvalumab IV), 1,500 mg
	Gemcitabine IV, 1,620 mg (1,000 mg/m2)
	Cisplatin IV, 41 mg (25 mg/m2)
	Sodium Chloride IV 0.9 %, 500 mL
	Magnesium Sulfate IV, 1 gm

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Durvalumab Trememilumab through May 2025
Cabozantinib 6/25-9/17/2025
Ivosednib 9/17

Pathology:
A. Aortocaval lymph node, biopsy: Positive for metastatic carcinoma. The morphological and staining findings are compatible with metastatic moderately to poorly differentiated HCC. 
    
   
   
History of Present Illness:
69 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC. Eventually with clinical and radiographic progression, switched to Ivosednib due to IDH-1 mutation 9/17/2025.

Interval History:

Patient reports return of constant abdominal pain since last night, described as diffuse abdominal and pelvic pain extending to the lower back near the spine. Pain was intolerable, prompting resumption of pain medication after being off for two months. Patient has been off ivosidenib since 12/30/2025 as directed. Reports weight of 127 lbs at home, representing continued weight stability. Bowel movements occurred twice yesterday, denies blood in stool. Denies fever, shortness of breath, or vomiting.

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hepatitis C
BPH

	Neoplasm related pain ( ICD-10:G89.3 ;Neoplasm related pain (acute) (chronic) )
	Primary carcinoma liver and/or biliary system (disorder) ( ICD-10:C24.8 ;Malignant neoplasm of overlapping sites of biliary tract )
	Thrombocytopenia ( ICD-10:D69.6 ;Thrombocytopenia, unspecified )
	Umbilical hernia (disorder) ( ICD-10:K42.9 ;Umbilical hernia without obstruction or gangrene )

Impression:
Guangul Zekiros is a 69-year-old male with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/CCA.

HCC vs mixed HCC/CCA:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC. Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass. His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 06/25/2025.  Review of CT late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression mid Sep 2025.

Of note, his tissue molecular profiling showed IDH-1, TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/HCC  especially that his circulating blood DNA showed a different profile with CTNNB1, TERT, CDKN1B Aberrancies. In light of that he was switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosidenib targeting mixed HCC/CCA. Commenced 09/17/2025. On 10/29/2025, imaging demonstrateed first evidence of disease stability with retroperitoneal mass measuring 6 x 3.5 cm compared to 6.2 x 3.7 cm on 09/17/2025, representing stabilization after multiple prior scans showing progression. Venous compression appeared improved with less narrowing, suggesting decreased mass effect. 11/24/2025 Patient continues on Ivosidenib therapy with significant clinical improvement including resolution of pain and weight stabilization with 1-pound gain to 121 lbs. Recent laboratory studies show normal kidney function, adequate protein levels, excellent liver function, and improved blood counts, supporting continued treatment response. 12/30/2025 Patient demonstrates paradoxical clinical course with continued symptomatic improvement including sustained pain relief off oxycodone for 10 days and further weight gain to 124.6 lbs, however imaging reveals clear disease progression with retroperitoneal mass enlargement to 7 x 3.85 cm and new areas of disease within liver and additional lymph nodes, indicating treatment failure with Ivosidenib requiring immediate treatment modification. 01/06/2026 Patient now presents with return of constant abdominal and pelvic pain extending to lower back, requiring resumption of pain medications. Currently off all anti-cancer therapy since discontinuation of ivosidenib on 12/30/2025. Had an extensive discussion about therapy plan. It appears he has different subclones of tumor cells supporting mixed histology. His prior disease progression on HCC-directed therapy were mainly extrahepatic that now is progressing after a period of disease stability on IDH-1 inhibitor alongside synchronous intrahepatic progression ( off HCC-directed therapy). I beleive best course of action is to transition to Gemcitabine Cisplatin Anti PD-1 regimen to cover progression in both disease elements. 

Plan:
Continue plan for gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab to address both hepatocellular carcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma components of mixed tumor.
Treatment schedule will consist of 3-week cycles with week 1 receiving all three agents, week 2 receiving chemotherapy agents only, and week 3 off treatment.
Arrange scheduling at alternative infusion center if needed to expedite treatment initiation once insurance approval obtained.
Provided comprehensive education regarding treatment expectations 
Pain management with resumption of analgesics as needed for current abdominal and pelvic discomfort.
Plan imaging reassessment approximately 8-12 weeks after treatment initiation to evaluate response.
Procedure:
   

This audio-video visit is being conducted through telemedicine on the VSee platform, is patient-initiated, and patient's consent was obtained.  The patient is currently located at home residence as defined by CMS, and provider is in the clinic.

.

Laith Abushahin MD

Chandana Reddy, D.O.

Date of Service: 01/22/2026

Referring Provider:
Dr. Amar Gupta

Chief Complaint:
Follow-up evaluation for mixed hepatocellular carcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma with initiation of gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab therapy.

Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( Mismatch Repair (MMR) status: Proficient; Microsatellite instability (MSI) status: Low; TRK gene: Negative; RET fusion status: Unknown; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Diagnosis*:
Treatment History:
Durvalumab + Gemcitabine D1,8 + Cisplatin D1,8 Q21D Cycle Length: 21 Number Cycles: 8 Start: C1D1 on 01/22/2026 Assoc Dx: Primary carcinoma liver and/or biliary system (disorder) LOT: Other Treatment Intent: Palliative01/12/2026 C1 D1

	Imfinzi (Durvalumab IV), 1,500 mg
	Gemcitabine IV, 1,620 mg (1,000 mg/m2)
	Cisplatin IV, 41 mg (25 mg/m2)
	Sodium Chloride IV 0.9 %, 500 mL
	Magnesium Sulfate IV, 1 gm

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Durvalumab Trememilumab through May 2025
Cabozantinib 6/25-9/17/2025
Ivosednib 9/17

Pathology:
A. Aortocaval lymph node, biopsy: Positive for metastatic carcinoma. The morphological and staining findings are compatible with metastatic moderately to poorly differentiated HCC. 
    
   
   
   
History of Present Illness:
69 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC. Eventually with clinical and radiographic progression, switched to Ivosednib due to IDH-1 mutation 9/17/2025. Eventually with extrahepatic and intrahepatic progression documented in Dec 2025. After extensive discussion commenced gem cisplatin durvalumab 1/22/2026

Interval History:
Patient reports return of constant abdominal and pelvic pain extending to the lower back since last night. Pain was intolerable, prompting resumption of oxycodone after being off pain medications for approximately 2 months. Patient notes constipation associated with pain medication use. He continues to work but reports significant fatigue and appears visibly thinner since the last visit. Patient has developed lower extremity edema with +1 pitting edema noted. He has been off ivosidenib since 12/30/2025 as directed. Currently taking oxycodone every 6-8 hours for pain management, previously taking it every 8 hours before discontinuation 2 months ago.

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hepatitis C
BPH

	Oxycodone Oral 5 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every 4 to 6 hours as needed for pain.

	Ivosidenib Oral 250 mg tablet 500 mg orally daily. Take with or without food. Avoid taking with a high-fat meal.
	Zyprexa (Olanzapine Oral) 2.5 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every evening.

	Sennosides-Docusate Sodium Oral 8.6 mg-50 mg 8.6-50 mg capsule 1 tablet or capsule orally 2 times per day as needed for constipation.

Medications reviewed and reconciled with patient.

No known medication allergies

 mixed HCC/CCA:

Planned for gem cis durvalumab

01/22/2026 Patient returns with worsening abdominal and pelvic pain extending to the lower back, appearing visibly thinner with new lower extremity edema and requiring resumption of opioid pain management after 2 months off medications. Started on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab regimen today to address both hepatocellular and cholangiocarcinoma components given evidence of progression in both tumor populations. Laboratory values remain reasonable supporting treatment initiation. Pain management optimized with addition of long-acting morphine and constipation prevention measures implemented.

Plan:
Initiated gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab therapy today to address both hepatocellular carcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma components of mixed tumor.
Treatment schedule consists of 3-week cycles with treatments on 01/22/2026, 01/29/2026, then one week off, followed by 02/12/2026.
Administered IV pain medication during today's treatment.
Added long-acting morphine for improved baseline pain control in addition to continuing oxycodone for breakthrough pain.
Prescribed Movantik for opioid-induced constipation management.
Recommend OTC docusate morning and evening with senna as needed for constipation prevention.
Prescribed olanzapine for chemotherapy-induced nausea prevention and appetite stimulation.
Discussed palliative care referral for comprehensive symptom management and potential improved outcomes.
Patient education provided regarding new treatment regimen, expected side effects including potential increased fatigue, and symptom monitoring.
Return precautions discussed regarding fever, severe nausea or vomiting, or worsening pain.

extracted.patient_update: Patient reports return of constant abdominal and pelvic pain extending to the lower back since last night. Pain was intolerable, prompting resumption of oxycodone after being off pain medications for approximately 2 months. Patient notes constipation associated with pain medication use. He continues to work but reports significant fatigue and appears visibly thinner since the last visit. Patient has developed lower extremity edema with +1 pitting edema noted. He has been off ivosidenib since 12/30/2025 as directed. Currently taking oxycodone every 6-8 hours for pain management, previously taking it every 8 hours before discontinuation 2 months ago.
extracted.dr_assessment: Guangul Zekiros is a 69-year-old male with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/CCA. mixed HCC/CCA: As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC. Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass. His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 06/25/2025. Review of CT late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression mid Sep 2025. Of note, his tissue molecular profiling showed IDH-1, TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/HCC especially that his circulating blood DNA showed a different profile with CTNNB1, TERT, CDKN1B Aberrancies. In light of that he was switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosidenib targeting mixed HCC/CCA. Commenced 09/17/2025. On 10/29/2025, imaging demonstrated first evidence of disease stability with retroperitoneal mass measuring 6 x 3.5 cm compared to 6.2 x 3.7 cm on 09/17/2025, representing stabilization after multiple prior scans showing progression. Venous compression appeared improved with less narrowing, suggesting decreased mass effect. 11/24/2025 Patient continues on Ivosidenib therapy with significant clinical improvement including resolution of pain and weight stabilization with 1-pound gain to 121 lbs. Recent laboratory studies show normal kidney function, adequate protein levels, excellent liver function, and improved blood counts, supporting continued treatment response. 12/30/2025 imaging revealed clear disease progression with retroperitoneal mass enlargement to 7 x 3.85 cm and new areas of disease within liver and additional lymph nodes, indicating treatment failure with Ivosidenib requiring immediate treatment modification. Planned for gem cis durvalumab 01/22/2026 Patient returns with worsening abdominal and pelvic pain extending to the lower back, appearing visibly thinner with new lower extremity edema and requiring resumption of opioid pain management after 2 months off medications. Started on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab regimen today to address both hepatocellular and cholangiocarcinoma components given evidence of progression in both tumor populations. Laboratory values remain reasonable supporting treatment initiation. Pain management optimized with addition of long-acting morphine and constipation prevention measures implemented.
extracted.next_steps: Initiated gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab therapy today to address both hepatocellular carcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma components of mixed tumor. Treatment schedule consists of 3-week cycles with treatments on 01/22/2026, 01/29/2026, then one week off, followed by 02/12/2026. Administered IV pain medication during today's treatment. Added long-acting morphine for improved baseline pain control in addition to continuing oxycodone for breakthrough pain. Prescribed Movantik for opioid-induced constipation management. Recommend OTC docusate morning and evening with senna as needed for constipation prevention. Prescribed olanzapine for chemotherapy-induced nausea prevention and appetite stimulation. Discussed palliative care referral for comprehensive symptom management and potential improved outcomes. Patient education provided regarding new treatment regimen, expected side effects including potential increased fatigue, and symptom monitoring. Return precautions discussed regarding fever, severe nausea or vomiting, or worsening pain. Follow-up scheduled for next week for cycle 1 day 8 treatment. Plan imaging reassessment approximately 8-12 weeks after treatment initiation to evaluate response.

Chief Complaint:
Follow-up evaluation for mixed hepatocellular carcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma with cycle 1 day 8 gemcitabine and cisplatin therapy.
Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( Mismatch Repair (MMR) status: Proficient; Microsatellite instability (MSI) status: Low; TRK gene: Negative; RET fusion status: Unknown; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Diagnosis*:
Treatment History:
Durvalumab + Gemcitabine D1,8 + Cisplatin D1,8 Q21D Cycle Length: 21 Number Cycles: 8 Start: C1D1 on 01/22/2026 Assoc Dx: Primary carcinoma liver and/or biliary system (disorder) LOT: Other Treatment Intent: Palliative01/12/2026 C1 D8

	Imfinzi (Durvalumab IV), 1,500 mg
	Gemcitabine IV, 1,620 mg (1,000 mg/m2)
	Cisplatin IV, 41 mg (25 mg/m2)
	Sodium Chloride IV 0.9 %, 500 mL
	Magnesium Sulfate IV, 1 gm

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Durvalumab Trememilumab through May 2025
Cabozantinib 6/25-9/17/2025
Ivosednib 9/17

Pathology:
A. Aortocaval lymph node, biopsy: Positive for metastatic carcinoma. The morphological and staining findings are compatible with metastatic moderately to poorly differentiated HCC. 
    
   
   
   
   
History of Present Illness:
69 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC. Eventually with clinical and radiographic progression, switched to Ivosednib due to IDH-1 mutation 9/17/2025. Eventually with extrahepatic and intrahepatic progression documented in Dec 2025. After extensive discussion commenced gem cisplatin durvalumab 1/22/2026

Interval History:
Patient reports several side effects since receiving first cycle of gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab on 01/22/2026. Most prominent symptom has been lower extremity swelling, with +1 pitting edema bilaterally that fluctuates throughout the day, more pronounced on the left side. Patient experienced significant hiccups in the first few days following treatment, with symptoms being most severe on Friday 01/24/2026 when he was unable to speak or sleep. Hiccups have resolved over the last 2 days. Patient also developed fresh, itchy rash on both legs. Reports ongoing fatigue since treatment. Appetite remains poor despite olanzapine prescription, though he has been inconsistent with daily dosing, most recently taking it on Wednesday 01/28/2026. Patient continues to experience constipation but reports good response to Movantik when taken regularly, with last bowel movement on Monday 01/26/2026. He denies fever, new cough, or shortness of breath. Pain management with morphine every 12 hours and oxycodone as needed remains adequate with breakthrough medication used approximately 3 times in the past week.

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hepatitis C
BPH

	Oxycodone Oral 5 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every 4 to 6 hours as needed for pain.
