Impression:
Guangul Zekiros is a 69-year-old male with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/CCA.

mixed HCC/CCA:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC. Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass. His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 06/25/2025. Review of CT late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression mid Sep 2025.

Of note, his tissue molecular profiling showed IDH-1, TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/HCC especially that his circulating blood DNA showed a different profile with CTNNB1, TERT, CDKN1B Aberrancies. In light of that he was switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosidenib targeting mixed HCC/CCA. Commenced 09/17/2025. On 10/29/2025, imaging demonstrated first evidence of disease stability with retroperitoneal mass measuring 6 x 3.5 cm compared to 6.2 x 3.7 cm on 09/17/2025, representing stabilization after multiple prior scans showing progression. Venous compression appeared improved with less narrowing, suggesting decreased mass effect. 11/24/2025 Patient continues on Ivosidenib therapy with significant clinical improvement including resolution of pain and weight stabilization with 1-pound gain to 121 lbs. Recent laboratory studies show normal kidney function, adequate protein levels, excellent liver function, and improved blood counts, supporting continued treatment response. 12/30/2025 imaging revealed clear disease progression with retroperitoneal mass enlargement to 7 x 3.85 cm and new areas of disease within liver and additional lymph nodes, indicating treatment failure with Ivosidenib requiring immediate treatment modification.

01/22/2026 Patient returned with worsening abdominal and pelvic pain extending to the lower back, appearing visibly thinner with new lower extremity edema and requiring resumption of opioid pain management after 2 months off medications. Started on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab regimen to address both hepatocellular and cholangiocarcinoma components given evidence of progression in both tumor populations.

01/29/2026 Patient presents for cycle 1 day 8 treatment with manageable toxicity profile including lower extremity edema, resolved hiccups, and fatigue. Creatinine elevation from 1.0 to 1.22 mg/dL requires monitoring and treatment modifications including dose reduction of cisplatin and addition of diuretic therapy. Treatment-related hiccups attributed to dexamethasone premedication prompting steroid substitution.

Plan:
Continue gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab therapy with modifications to address treatment-related toxicities.
Switch premedication steroid from dexamethasone to methylprednisolone to prevent recurrent hiccups.
Administered slightly reduced cisplatin dose today to minimize kidney toxicity given creatinine elevation.
Provided Baclofen prescription to pharmacy for hiccup management as needed, up to 3 times daily.
Started spironolactone for fluid retention management, to be taken every other day rather than daily to minimize kidney stress.
Emphasized importance of taking olanzapine daily every evening for appetite stimulation rather than only for nausea.
Continue current pain management regimen with morphine every 12 hours and oxycodone as needed for breakthrough pain.
Continue Movantik and Senokot for constipation management as currently effective.
Patient education provided regarding medication changes and importance of daily olanzapine compliance.
Return precautions discussed regarding fever, severe nausea or vomiting, or worsening symptoms.
Scheduled return visit next Friday 02/05/2026 for kidney function monitoring and laboratory assessment.
Next treatment cycle scheduled for 02/12/2026 pending acceptable laboratory values.
Procedure:
Gemcitabine and Cisplatin Infusion
Patient received cycle 1 day 8 treatment with gemcitabine and cisplatin. Cisplatin dose slightly reduced to minimize kidney toxicity. Premedications modified to replace dexamethasone with methylprednisolone due to hiccup side effects from previous treatment.

.

Laith Abushahin MD

Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

extracted.patient_update: Patient reports several side effects since receiving first cycle of gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab on 01/22/2026. Most prominent symptom has been lower extremity swelling, with +1 pitting edema bilaterally that fluctuates throughout the day, more pronounced on the left side. Patient experienced significant hiccups in the first few days following treatment, with symptoms being most severe on Friday 01/24/2026 when he was unable to speak or sleep. Hiccups have resolved over the last 2 days. Patient also developed fresh, itchy rash on both legs. Reports ongoing fatigue since treatment. Appetite remains poor despite olanzapine prescription, though he has been inconsistent with daily dosing, most recently taking it on Wednesday 01/28/2026. Patient continues to experience constipation but reports good response to Movantik when taken regularly, with last bowel movement on Monday 01/26/2026. He denies fever, new cough, or shortness of breath. Pain management with morphine every 12 hours and oxycodone as needed remains adequate with breakthrough medication used approximately 3 times in the past week.
extracted.dr_assessment: Guangul Zekiros is a 69-year-old male with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/CCA. mixed HCC/CCA: As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC. Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass. His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 06/25/2025. Review of CT late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression mid Sep 2025. Of note, his tissue molecular profiling showed IDH-1, TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/HCC especially that his circulating blood DNA showed a different profile with CTNNB1, TERT, CDKN1B Aberrancies. In light of that he was switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosidenib targeting mixed HCC/CCA. Commenced 09/17/2025. On 10/29/2025, imaging demonstrated first evidence of disease stability with retroperitoneal mass measuring 6 x 3.5 cm compared to 6.2 x 3.7 cm on 09/17/2025, representing stabilization after multiple prior scans showing progression. Venous compression appeared improved with less narrowing, suggesting decreased mass effect. 11/24/2025 Patient continues on Ivosidenib therapy with significant clinical improvement including resolution of pain and weight stabilization with 1-pound gain to 121 lbs. Recent laboratory studies show normal kidney function, adequate protein levels, excellent liver function, and improved blood counts, supporting continued treatment response. 12/30/2025 imaging revealed clear disease progression with retroperitoneal mass enlargement to 7 x 3.85 cm and new areas of disease within liver and additional lymph nodes, indicating treatment failure with Ivosidenib requiring immediate treatment modification. 01/22/2026 Patient returned with worsening abdominal and pelvic pain extending to the lower back, appearing visibly thinner with new lower extremity edema and requiring resumption of opioid pain management after 2 months off medications. Started on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab regimen to address both hepatocellular and cholangiocarcinoma components given evidence of progression in both tumor populations. 01/29/2026 Patient presents for cycle 1 day 8 treatment with manageable toxicity profile including lower extremity edema, resolved hiccups, and fatigue. Creatinine elevation from 1.0 to 1.22 mg/dL requires monitoring and treatment modifications including dose reduction of cisplatin and addition of diuretic therapy. Treatment-related hiccups attributed to dexamethasone premedication prompting steroid substitution.
extracted.next_steps: Continue gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab therapy with modifications to address treatment-related toxicities. Switch premedication steroid from dexamethasone to methylprednisolone to prevent recurrent hiccups. Administered slightly reduced cisplatin dose today to minimize kidney toxicity given creatinine elevation. Provided Baclofen prescription to pharmacy for hiccup management as needed, up to 3 times daily. Started spironolactone for fluid retention management, to be taken every other day rather than daily to minimize kidney stress. Emphasized importance of taking olanzapine daily every evening for appetite stimulation rather than only for nausea. Continue current pain management regimen with morphine every 12 hours and oxycodone as needed for breakthrough pain. Continue Movantik and Senokot for constipation management as currently effective. Patient education provided regarding medication changes and importance of daily olanzapine compliance. Return precautions discussed regarding fever, severe nausea or vomiting, or worsening symptoms. Scheduled return visit next Friday 02/05/2026 for kidney function monitoring and laboratory assessment. Next treatment cycle scheduled for 02/12/2026 pending acceptable laboratory values.

Referring Provider:
Dr. Amar Gupta

Chief Complaint:
Follow-up evaluation for mixed hepatocellular carcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma with cycle 2 gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab therapy.
Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( Mismatch Repair (MMR) status: Proficient; Microsatellite instability (MSI) status: Low; TRK gene: Negative; RET fusion status: Unknown; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Diagnosis*:
Treatment History:
Durvalumab + Gemcitabine D1,8 + Cisplatin D1,8 Q21D Cycle Length: 21 Number Cycles: 8 Start: C1D1 on 01/22/2026 Assoc Dx: Primary carcinoma liver and/or biliary system (disorder) LOT: Other Treatment Intent: Palliative01/12/2026 C1 D8

	Imfinzi (Durvalumab IV), 1,500 mg
	Gemcitabine IV, 1,620 mg (1,000 mg/m2)
	Cisplatin IV, 41 mg (25 mg/m2)
	Sodium Chloride IV 0.9 %, 500 mL
	Magnesium Sulfate IV, 1 gm

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Durvalumab Trememilumab through May 2025
Cabozantinib 6/25-9/17/2025
Ivosednib 9/17

Pathology:
A. Aortocaval lymph node, biopsy: Positive for metastatic carcinoma. The morphological and staining findings are compatible with metastatic moderately to poorly differentiated HCC. 
   
History of Present Illness:
69 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC. Eventually with clinical and radiographic progression, switched to Ivosednib due to IDH-1 mutation 9/17/2025. Eventually with extrahepatic and intrahepatic progression documented in Dec 2025. After extensive discussion commenced gem cisplatin durvalumab 1/22/2026

Interval History:
Patient continues to experience lower extremity swelling as primary concern since last cycle, particularly affecting feet with fluctuation throughout the day. Swelling returns when legs are not elevated, and patient reports inability to maintain leg elevation for entire day. Fatigue continues as second major symptom. Appetite has improved compared to last visit and patient is making efforts to eat. Hiccups that occurred following first cycle have completely resolved after switching premedication steroids from dexamethasone to methylprednisolone. Patient did not require the prescribed hiccup medication. Reports no fevers or vomiting since last treatment. Pain levels remain stable without deterioration. Has not used morphine in several weeks. No constipation reported.

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hepatitis C
BPH   
 
Medications:

	Morphine Oral ER Tab 15 mg tablet extended release 1 tablet extended release orally every 12 hours.
	Tamsulosin Oral 0.4 mg capsule 1 capsule PO daily
	Zyprexa (Olanzapine Oral) 2.5 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every evening.
	Omeprazole Oral Delayed Release Capsule 20 mg capsule,delayed release(DR/EC) po daily
	Sennosides-Docusate Sodium Oral 8.6 mg-50 mg 8.6-50 mg capsule 1 tablet or capsule orally 2 times per day as needed for constipation.
	Spironolactone Oral 50 mg tablet Every other day
	Baclofen Oral 10 mg tablet 1 tablet orally 3 times per day as needed. hiccups
	Oxycodone Oral 5 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every 4 to 6 hours as needed for pain.
	Entecavir Oral 0.5 mg tablet q d
	Spironolactone Oral 50 mg tablet 1 tablet orally daily. Every other day
	Movantik (Naloxegol Oral) 25 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every morning. must be taken on empty stomach; no food 1 hr after or 2-3 hrs before dose.

Medications reviewed and reconciled with patient.

Allergies:
No known medication allergies

Smoking Status:
Smoking Tobacco : Never smoker; Smokeless Tobacco : none found; Vaping : none found

Social History:
Patient from Ethiopia originally.  Has 1 son and 1 daughter.  Never smoker but currently vaping.

	Mother: Deceased

	Brother 2: Alive and Well

12 point ROS reviewed with pt and was negatives with the exception of pain  as above.

HEENT: Normocephalic. Atraumatic. No oral lesions. Oral cavity is clear.
Lungs: No increased work of breathing. No use of accessory muscles.
Heart: Good peripheral perfusion.
Abdomen: Soft. Nondistended. No swelling. Nontender to deep palpation.
Extremities: Lower extremity edema present bilaterally, particularly in feet. No cyanosis.
Neurological: No focal deficits appreciated.

Psychiatric: Good judgment. Normal affect.

Impression:
Guangul Zekiros is a 69-year-old male with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/CCA.

Mixed HCC/CCA:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC. Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass. His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 06/25/2025. Review of CT late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression mid Sep 2025. Of note, his tissue molecular profiling showed IDH-1, TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/HCC especially that his circulating blood DNA showed a different profile with CTNNB1, TERT, CDKN1B Aberrancies. In light of that he was switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosidenib targeting mixed HCC/CCA. Commenced 09/17/2025. On 10/29/2025, imaging demonstrated first evidence of disease stability with retroperitoneal mass measuring 6 x 3.5 cm compared to 6.2 x 3.7 cm on 09/17/2025, representing stabilization after multiple prior scans showing progression. Venous compression appeared improved with less narrowing, suggesting decreased mass effect. 11/24/2025 Patient continues on Ivosidenib therapy with significant clinical improvement including resolution of pain and weight stabilization with 1-pound gain to 121 lbs. Recent laboratory studies show normal kidney function, adequate protein levels, excellent liver function, and improved blood counts, supporting continued treatment response. 12/30/2025 imaging revealed clear disease progression with retroperitoneal mass enlargement to 7 x 3.85 cm and new areas of disease within liver and additional lymph nodes, indicating treatment failure with Ivosidenib requiring immediate treatment modification. 01/22/2026 Patient returned with worsening abdominal and pelvic pain extending to the lower back, appearing visibly thinner with new lower extremity edema and requiring resumption of opioid pain management after 2 months off medications. Started on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab regimen to address both hepatocellular and cholangiocarcinoma components given evidence of progression in both tumor populations. 01/29/2026 Patient presents for cycle 1 day 8 treatment with manageable toxicity profile including lower extremity edema, resolved hiccups, and fatigue. Creatinine elevation from 1.0 to 1.22 mg/dL requires monitoring and treatment modifications including dose reduction of cisplatin and addition of diuretic therapy. Treatment-related hiccups attributed to dexamethasone premedication prompting steroid substitution. 02/12/2026 Patient presents for cycle 2 with continued management of treatment-related toxicities. Lower extremity edema remains primary concern with complete resolution of hiccups following steroid modification. Laboratory studies demonstrate normalization of creatinine to baseline levels and continued improvement in liver function markers including decreasing alkaline phosphatase since treatment initiation. Pain remains well-controlled without morphine requirement over past several weeks. Appetite showing improvement with patient actively working to maintain nutrition. Clinical stability noted with absence of disease progression symptoms and encouraging laboratory trends supporting treatment response.

Plan:
Continue gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab therapy with current regimen showing stable disease and improving laboratory markers.
Recommend compression stockings as initial management for lower extremity edema before considering additional diuretic therapy.
Continue spironolactone, consider adding Lasix next month if compression stockings insufficient.
Encourage increased protein intake to improve oncotic pressure and fluid management given low albumin level of 2.4.
Plan imaging assessment after current cycle or subsequent cycle to evaluate treatment response.
Provided patient education on compression stocking use including appropriate pressure ranges.
Continue current pain management regimen given stable pain control without morphine requirement over past several weeks.
Monitor laboratory trends including liver function and renal function with next cycle.
Procedure:
   

Laith Abushahin MD

Chandana Reddy, D.O.

69 yo male with mixed HCC and cholangiocarcinoma with mets to the liver, lymph nodes, and retroperitoneum, currently on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab, who presents to palliative care for pain and symptom mgmt.  

Patient seen over telemedicine for his initial palliative care consultation with his son Fanuel. 
He is feeling severe fatigue, no appetite at all and has not eaten all day.  No power or energy to get up and move around.  He has a little bit of nausea that comes and goes.  He does not throw up.  He thinks it is the nausea that is preventing him from eating.  He is losing any weight.  He takes olanzapine every night but that is not helping.  He does not take any additional nausea meds. He had diarrhea 6-7x per day and took imodium after a protein drink.  He takes senna and movantik as needed for OIC.  Lately, he just has diarrhea.  
He also has swelling in both feet. He is on spironolactone and recently added lasix.  He is also using compression socks but the swelling is back and forth. right now, it is good. He has testicular swelling.  That makes it difficult for him to walk without support.  He is urinating normal on the medicines.  
No pain.  He takes MSER BID which has resolved the pain.  
He will be on the couch in the day. Can ambulate around house without devices.  

He feels fine on his treatment except fatigue and nausea.

IMPRESSION: CHEST: 1. Increased size of an indeterminate subcentimeter right lower lobe nodule. Recommend attention on short-term follow-up CT chest reevaluation. 2. No thoracic lymphadenopathy. ABDOMEN AND PELVIS: 1. Progression of local tumor recurrence within the left hepatic lobe, with new/worsening multifocal hepatic metastatic disease. New probable tumor thrombus within the right posterior portal vein branch. 2. Progression of extensive infiltrative nodal and peritoneal metastatic disease. 3. Trace ascites.

	Thrombocytopenia ( ICD-10:D69.6 ;Thrombocytopenia, unspecified )

Assessment and Plan:
69 yo male with mixed HCC and cholangiocarcinoma with mets to the liver, lymph nodes, and retroperitoneum, currently on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab, who presents to palliative care for pain and symptom mgmt.  

1.  Severe Nausea, worse with chemotherapy.  It is not better with olanzapine 2.5 mg.  Son says he has been on this for weeks.  
--He is premed with chemo with Palonestron and Fosaprepitant.  Also getting solumedrol 60 mg IV.  
PLAN:  INCREASE Olanzapine to 5 mg QHS. 
--START Ondansetron ODT 4 mg Q6 hours PRN. 
--May also use Ginger candies PRN.  

2.  Severe loss of appetite, not eating at all and abnormal weight loss - Albumin is 2.2 now.  
--Discussed that loss of appetite is normal in advanced cancer and that it is very challenging to eat.  Discussed there is only moderate evidence for a couple of medications for appetite stimulation which include steroids (not good long term), megace (risk of blood clots).  Other medications are not effective and therefore, this is a challenging symptom to treat.  Reviewed that expectation should not be to eat normal or gain weight, but to eat enough, get good nutrition, and keep weight stable. 
--He is doing peptide drinks which have 500 cal and 24 g protein each.  
PLAN:  Recommended small frequent meals every 3-4 hours with protein at each meal and 2 protein shakes per day to improve caloric intake. Need to eat on a timer Q3-4 hours since no hunger signs or body ques telling you to eat.  
--Reviewed goals of 2,000 cal per day and 80 grams per day.  
--He is on olanzapine and increasing the dose as above. 
--Reviewed option for megace but 30% chance risk of DVT/PE.  They do not want to proceed with that right now, even though this does work.
--Reviewed option for cannabis but he is home alone and that can be concerning for confusion and disorientation, fall risk, etc.  

3.  Severe Cancer Related Fatigue - 
--Reviewed option to trial Ritalin PRN for extra boost in energy.  Each pill is immediate release and lasts for 4-6 hours.  May take dose in AM and repeat at lunch but none past 2 pm so he can sleep. Side effects include Htn, tachycardia, jitteriness/anxiety, poor sleep/restlessness, dry mouth and can suppress appetite. 
PLAN:  We are not pursuing ritalin due to his severe loss of appetite.
Can try caffeine.

4.  Bilateral leg swelling due to severe hypoalbuminemia.  Currently albumin is 2.2.
PLAN:  He is on lasix and spironolactone.  
Reviewed the necessity to raise albumin level closer to 3 to help this.  

5. Cancer Related Pain due to cancer in liver.  Currently controlled on his regimen.  
--CT scans reviewed to correlated site of pain with its source as documented above.
Total OME= 30 mg.  
--PDMP reviewed. Prescribed MSER 15 mg #60 on 2/19 by Dr Abushahin. Prior oxy IR 5 mg in Sept 2025. 
PLAN: Continue MSER 15 mg BID
He is not on any breakthrough pain meds. 

6. Drug/Opioid Induced Constipation- All pain medications cause constipation, so it is very important for you to take a daily bowel regimen. The goal is to have a BM every day or at least every other day. 
PLAN:  Please take Miralax one capful in 8 ounces of liquid AND Sennakot 15 mg tablets once daily.  Increase to twice daily if needed to have regular BMs.  He may substitute colace for miralax if not able to drink the quantity of liquid. 
--If you remain constipated, you may change to Sennakot to Bisacodyl 10 mg once or twice daily.  
--He has Movantik but reserved for last resort.  
--If this is ineffective or no BM within 3 days, call the clinic.   All of these medications are over the counter.  
 
7. Mixed HCC and cholangiocarcinoma with mets to the liver, lymph nodes, and retroperitoneum, currently on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab
--Onc notes were reviewed. 
--Role of palliative care in oncology was epxlained.  

Psychosocial, Emotional and Spiritual:

Advance Care Planning and Goals of Care Discussion:

Advance Care Planning Documents:
None completed. 

.
Follow up in 1 month. 

Stephanie Y. Terauchi MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Dr Laith Abushahin

Pathology:
A. Aortocaval lymph node, biopsy: Positive for metastatic carcinoma. The morphological and staining findings are compatible with metastatic moderately to poorly differentiated HCC. 
   
   
History of Present Illness:
69 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC. Eventually with clinical and radiographic progression, switched to Ivosednib due to IDH-1 mutation 9/17/2025. Eventually with extrahepatic and intrahepatic progression documented in Dec 2025. After extensive discussion commenced gem cisplatin durvalumab 1/22/2026

Interval History:
Patient continues with well-controlled pain on morphine twice daily. Fatigue remains the primary concern, causing him to stay home more frequently. He experiences nausea and takes olanzapine as needed, which is helpful. He denies any vomiting episodes since last visit. Patient is taking liquid protein supplement for nutrition but this causes significant diarrhea within approximately 15 minutes of consumption. He has lost 12 lbs since last visit, now weighing 117 lbs. Current diuretic regimen with Lasix 40 mg daily and spironolactone is working effectively, though provider is concerned about potential over-diuresis given the significant weight loss. He reports no shortness of breath, joint pain, or skin rash. He denies fever or other concerning symptoms.

	Spironolactone Oral 25 mg tablet 1 tablet orally daily. Every other day

	Lasix (Furosemide Oral) 20 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every morning.

	Oxycodone Oral 5 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every 4 to 6 hours as needed for pain.
	Omeprazole Oral Delayed Release Capsule 20 mg capsule,delayed release(DR/EC) po daily

	Olanzapine Oral 5 mg tablet

	Lasix (Furosemide Oral) 20 mg tablet 1 tablet orally every morning.

Medications reviewed and reconciled with patient.

No known medication allergies

Smoking Tobacco : Never smoker; Smokeless Tobacco : none found; Vaping : none found

Social History:
Patient from Ethiopia originally.  Has 1 son and 1 daughter.  Never smoker but currently vaping.

	Brother 2: Alive and Well
	Sister 1: Deceased
	Sister 2: Alive and Well - Cerebrovascular accident, Hypertension

ROS:

Impression:
Guangul Zekiros is a 69-year-old male with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/CCA.

Mixed HCC/CCA:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC. Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass. His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 06/25/2025. Review of CT late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression mid Sep 2025. Of note, his tissue molecular profiling showed IDH-1, TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/HCC especially that his circulating blood DNA showed a different profile with CTNNB1, TERT, CDKN1b Aberrancies. In light of that he was switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosidenib targeting mixed HCC/CCA. Commenced 09/17/2025. On 10/29/2025, imaging demonstrated first evidence of disease stability with retroperitoneal mass measuring 6 x 3.5 cm compared to 6.2 x 3.7 cm on 09/17/2025, representing stabilization after multiple prior scans showing progression. Venous compression appeared improved with less narrowing, suggesting decreased mass effect. 11/24/2025 Patient continues on Ivosidenib therapy with significant clinical improvement including resolution of pain and weight stabilization with 1-pound gain to 121 lbs. Recent laboratory studies show normal kidney function, adequate protein levels, excellent liver function, and improved blood counts, supporting continued treatment response. 12/30/2025 imaging revealed clear disease progression with retroperitoneal mass enlargement to 7 x 3.85 cm and new areas of disease within liver and additional lymph nodes, indicating treatment failure with Ivosidenib requiring immediate treatment modification. 01/22/2026 Patient returned with worsening abdominal and pelvic pain extending to the lower back, appearing visibly thinner with new lower extremity edema and requiring resumption of opioid pain management after 2 months off medications. Started on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab regimen to address both hepatocellular and cholangiocarcinoma components given evidence of progression in both tumor populations. 01/29/2026 Patient presents for cycle 1 day 8 treatment with manageable toxicity profile including lower extremity edema, resolved hiccups, and fatigue. Creatinine elevation from 1.0 to 1.22 mg/dL requires monitoring and treatment modifications including dose reduction of cisplatin and addition of diuretic therapy. Treatment-related hiccups attributed to dexamethasone premedication prompting steroid substitution. 02/12/2026 Patient presents for cycle 2 with continued management of treatment-related toxicities. Lower extremity edema remains primary concern with complete resolution of hiccups following steroid modification. Laboratory studies demonstrate normalization of creatinine to baseline levels and continued improvement in liver function markers including decreasing alkaline phosphatase since treatment initiation. Pain remains well-controlled without morphine requirement over past several weeks. Appetite showing improvement with patient actively working to maintain nutrition. Clinical stability noted with absence of disease progression symptoms and encouraging laboratory trends supporting treatment response.

03/05/2026 Patient continues on current treatment regimen with excellent pain control on morphine twice daily. Primary concern remains fatigue limiting daily activities. Laboratory studies demonstrate continued improvement in liver function with alkaline phosphatase further decreasing to 190 and protein levels improving to 2.4. Significant 12-pound weight loss to 117 lbs raises concern for over-diuresis with current Lasix and spironolactone regimen. Patient experiencing treatment-related nausea managed with olanzapine as needed. Diarrhea related to liquid protein supplement intake requires management with Imodium. Blood counts remain adequate for continued treatment.

Plan:
Continue gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab therapy with current regimen showing stable disease and improving laboratory markers.
Reduce Lasix and spironolactone doses by half given significant 12-pound weight loss and concern for over-diuresis.
Take Imodium concurrent with liquid protein supplement to manage diarrhea.
Discussed appetite stimulant options but will down prioritize megestrol acetate due to increased thrombotic risk given patient's hypercoagulable state from cancer and retroperitoneal mass causing venous compression.
Provided refills for Lasix, spironolactone, and olanzapine.
Adjust chemotherapy dosing for current weight of 117 lbs.
Schedule surveillance imaging to assess treatment response.
Return if develops shortness of breath, joint pain, rash, or other concerning symptoms.
Follow up in 1 week for next treatment cycle.
Procedure:
   

Laith Abushahin MD

   
Send copy of note to:
Chandana Reddy, D.O.
.

.

Interval History:
Patient seen over telemedicine for FU with his son and daughter.  
He is taking olanzapine every night and he has no more nausea.  Appetite has improved.  Olanzapine seems to be helping.  He is 2.5 meals per day.  He only had one episode of pain that went away.  He is still taking MSER 15 mg BID.  He took only 1 tablet per day x 2 days then he had pain.  So went back to BID dosing.   He is not constipated. Has BM once per day.  He is not using any laxatives.  
He still lays in bed all day. He will walk inside house a little bit.  He does not have much motivation to get up.

Assessment and Plan:

69 yo male with mixed HCC and cholangiocarcinoma with mets to the liver, lymph nodes, and retroperitoneum, currently on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab with PET 3/13 showing ongoing POD and large volume ascites, who presents to palliative care for pain and symptom mgmt.  

1.  Severe Nausea, worse with chemotherapy.  It is not better with olanzapine 2.5 mg.  Son says he has been on this for weeks.  
--He is premed with chemo with Palonestron and Fosaprepitant.  Also getting solumedrol 60 mg IV.  
PLAN:  Continue Olanzapine to 5 mg QHS. This has resolved his nausea.  
--Continue Ondansetron ODT 4 mg Q6 hours PRN. Not needed.  
--May also use Ginger candies PRN.  

2.  Severe loss of appetite, not eating at all and abnormal weight loss - Albumin is 2.2 now.  
--Discussed that loss of appetite is normal in advanced cancer and that it is very challenging to eat.  Discussed there is only moderate evidence for a couple of medications for appetite stimulation which include steroids (not good long term), megace (risk of blood clots).  Other medications are not effective and therefore, this is a challenging symptom to treat.  Reviewed that expectation should not be to eat normal or gain weight, but to eat enough, get good nutrition, and keep weight stable. 
--He is doing peptide drinks which have 500 cal and 24 g protein each.  
PLAN:  Recommended small frequent meals every 3-4 hours with protein at each meal and 2 protein shakes per day to improve caloric intake. Need to eat on a timer Q3-4 hours since no hunger signs or body ques telling you to eat.  
--Reviewed goals of 2,000 cal per day and 80 grams per day.    He is eating better but does not know his weight.  
--He is on olanzapine and increasing the dose as above. He says this has helped his appetite.  
--He is NOT a candidate for megace given tumor thrombus and risk of further thromboembolism. 
--Reviewed option for cannabis but he is home alone and that can be concerning for confusion and disorientation, fall risk, etc.  

3.  Severe Cancer Related Fatigue - 
--Reviewed option to trial Ritalin PRN for extra boost in energy.  Each pill is immediate release and lasts for 4-6 hours.  May take dose in AM and repeat at lunch but none past 2 pm so he can sleep. Side effects include Htn, tachycardia, jitteriness/anxiety, poor sleep/restlessness, dry mouth and can suppress appetite. 
PLAN:  We are not pursuing ritalin due to his severe loss of appetite.
Can try caffeine.  
Encouraged more activity.   

4.  Bilateral leg swelling due to severe hypoalbuminemia.  Currently albumin is 2.2.
PLAN:  He is on lasix and spironolactone.  Not managed by me - onc has written scripts and he has a renal doctor.  
Reviewed the necessity to raise albumin level closer to 3 to help this.  

5. Cancer Related Pain due to cancer in liver.  Currently controlled on his regimen.  
--CT scans reviewed to correlated site of pain with its source as documented above.
Total OME= 30 mg.  
--PDMP reviewed. Prescribed MSER 15 mg #60 on 2/19 by Dr Abushahin. Prior oxy IR 5 mg in Sept 2025. 
PLAN: Continue MSER 15 mg BID - pain stable on this dosing.  Refilled yday.  
He is not on any breakthrough pain meds. 

6. Drug/Opioid Induced Constipation- All pain medications cause constipation, so it is very important for you to take a daily bowel regimen. The goal is to have a BM every day or at least every other day. 
PLAN:  Please take Miralax one capful in 8 ounces of liquid AND Sennakot 15 mg tablets once daily.  Increase to twice daily if needed to have regular BMs.  He may substitute colace for miralax if not able to drink the quantity of liquid. 
--If you remain constipated, you may change to Sennakot to Bisacodyl 10 mg once or twice daily.  
--He has Movantik but reserved for last resort.  
--If this is ineffective or no BM within 3 days, call the clinic.   All of these medications are over the counter.  
 
7. Mixed HCC and cholangiocarcinoma with mets to the liver, lymph nodes, and retroperitoneum, currently on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab. PET 3/13 showed POD and waiting on onc comments.  
--Onc notes were reviewed. 
--Role of palliative care in oncology was explained.  

Psychosocial, Emotional and Spiritual:

.

Chief Complaint:
Follow-up evaluation for mixed hepatocellular carcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma with ongoing gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab therapy.
Principal Diagnosis:

	Hepatocellular carcinoma ( Mismatch Repair (MMR) status: Proficient; Microsatellite instability (MSI) status: Low; TRK gene: Negative; RET fusion status: Unknown; ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )
	Hepatoma ( ICD-10:C22.0 ;Liver cell carcinoma )

Diagnosis*:
Treatment History:
Durvalumab + Gemcitabine D1,8 + Cisplatin D1,8 Q21D Cycle Length: 21 Number Cycles: 8 Start: C1D1 on 01/22/2026 Assoc Dx: Primary carcinoma liver and/or biliary system (disorder) LOT: Other Treatment Intent: Palliative01/12/2026 C1 D8

Treatment History*:
Emergent particle and coil embolization for ruptured hepatic mass on 10/13/2024.
Durvalumab Trememilumab through May 2025
Cabozantinib 6/25-9/17/2025
Ivosednib 9/17

Pathology:
A. Aortocaval lymph node, biopsy: Positive for metastatic carcinoma. The morphological and staining findings are compatible with metastatic moderately to poorly differentiated HCC. 
   
   
   
History of Present Illness:
69 y.o. male with a past medical history of HTN, BPH, remote HCV (treated) who presented 10/12/2024 with severe abdominal pain; Pain was severe, started after picking up a heavy object, localized to the upper quadrants of his abdomen with radiation to the ribcage; was continuous and associated with hypotension and near-syncope. In the ED, he was hypotensive; responded to IVF crystalloid resuscitation. Lab data with Hgb 11.8. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast revealed a 7.1 cm left hepatic lobe mass with evidence of active extravasation associated with small-volume hemoperitoneum. Pt was emergently taken to the IR suite and underwent angiography on 10/13/2024.  During procedure, A large hypervascular tumor primarily supplied by the left hepatic artery with foci of active contrast extravasation was noted. Proximal left hepatic artery bland embolization was performed utilizing 100-300 um beads. Contrast was then injected through the microcatheter for post embolization angiography, noting persistent active bleeding into the tumor from a small branch of the left hepatic artery. The microcatheter was positioned within the culprit artery and coil embolization performed utilizing a single 2 mm x 3 cm Nester coil followed by additional bland embolization until stasis was achieved with absent tumor enhancement after embolization.  He remained hospitalized until 10/18/2024 with significant abdominal pain and intractable nausea and vomiting that eventually resolved with supportive management.  Imaging with liver MRI 10/13 and 10/18 as well as abdominal CTA on 10/17 showed no further bleeding with features of unifocal 7.6 cm treated tumor in segment 4b with possible residual nonnodular area along the superior anterior margin.  Met with medical Oncology and counseled extensively that the presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB although retrospective reports suggested that could be an overestimation with some patients possibly attaining good outcomes. BCLC classification does not take rupture into account but based on clinical presentation this could either be either BCLC- B ( intermediate stage ) vs BCLC-C ( Advanced) if he truly has peritoneal seeding from rupture. In light of that and a positive CtDNA test,  he was started on systemic therapy with dual immunotherapy on 11/11/2024. Eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval node confirmed with biopsy. switched to Cabozantinib 6/25/2025. Molecular profiling questionable for mixed HCC/iCC. Eventually with clinical and radiographic progression, switched to Ivosednib due to IDH-1 mutation 9/17/2025. Eventually with extrahepatic and intrahepatic progression documented in Dec 2025. After extensive discussion commenced gem cisplatin durvalumab 1/22/2026

Interval History:
Patient reports increased pain and fatigue today, requiring wheelchair for mobility. Weight remains stable at 118 lbs. He continues eating well including protein supplements (peptide), currently taking 1.5 supplements daily with goal to increase to 2. He denies fevers since treatment on March 5th. His counts were too low on March 12th for scheduled treatment. Fatigue remains his primary concern, with today's increased symptoms attributed to poor sleep from frequent nighttime urination. Bowel movements are normal, occurring daily with solid consistency. He denies lower extremity swelling, nausea, or vomiting.

Impression:
Guangul Zekiros is a 69-year-old male with previous medical history of hepatitis C and hypertension who presented with a suspected ruptured unifocal HCC, eventually with evidence of progression in aortocaval nodes with sampling raising concern of mixed type HCC/CCA.

Mixed HCC/CCA:
As detailed above, known history of HCV and cirrhosis putting him at risk of developing HCC. Presented with a large hyper vascular left liver lobe lesion with rupture and hemoperitoneum with angiographic and MRI features indicative of HCC. Underwent emergent left hepatic artery embolization which stabilized him clinically and provided liver directed intervention for the mass. His Child score was 6. CLIP score likely 0. The presentation with rupture is classified as T4 per TNM staging placing it as at least stage IIIB. Liquid biopsy on 10/21/2024 showed circulating tumor DNA suggestive of residual disease. In light of that he commenced systemic therapy on 11/11/2024. He remained on immunotherapy through May 2025. Serial scans showed improvement of residual disease in liver but worsening retroperitoneal perivascular infiltration. Eventually underwent robotic laparoscopic incisional biopsy confirming malignancy with pathology suggestion of poorly differentiated HCC as cells where AFP and cytokeratin positive while negative of mostly everything else. He was switched to second line Cabozantinib which was started 06/25/2025. Review of CT late August 2025 showed clearly it is worse than the scan in May 2025. what was unclear is if it represented true progression through Cabozantinib vs pre therapy uncaptured progression as his scans in May were about 35 days before treatment initiation. The description of the pain appeared to support the latter. For that we continued Cabozantinib 40 mg po daily and monitored carefully clinically and radiographically. Eventually with persistent clinical symptoms suggestive of disease progression mid Sep 2025. Of note, his tissue molecular profiling showed IDH-1, TP53, and ARID2 mutations which raised suspicion of mixed type chlangio/HCC especially that his circulating blood DNA showed a different profile with CTNNB1, TERT, CDKN1b Aberrancies. In light of that he was switched to 3rd line treatment with Ivosidenib targeting mixed HCC/CCA. Commenced 09/17/2025. On 10/29/2025, imaging demonstrated first evidence of disease stability with retroperitoneal mass measuring 6 x 3.5 cm compared to 6.2 x 3.7 cm on 09/17/2025, representing stabilization after multiple prior scans showing progression. Venous compression appeared improved with less narrowing, suggesting decreased mass effect. 11/24/2025 Patient continues on Ivosidenib therapy with significant clinical improvement including resolution of pain and weight stabilization with 1-pound gain to 121 lbs. Recent laboratory studies show normal kidney function, adequate protein levels, excellent liver function, and improved blood counts, supporting continued treatment response. 12/29/2025 imaging revealed clear disease progression with retroperitoneal mass enlargement to 7 x 3.85 cm and new areas of disease within liver and additional lymph nodes, indicating treatment failure with Ivosidenib requiring immediate treatment modification. 01/22/2026 Patient returned with worsening abdominal and pelvic pain extending to the lower back, appearing visibly thinner with new lower extremity edema and requiring resumption of opioid pain management after 2 months off medications. Started on gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab regimen to address both hepatocellular and cholangiocarcinoma components given evidence of progression in both tumor populations.

03/26/2026 Patient presents with increased pain and fatigue requiring wheelchair assistance. Treatment held on 03/12/2026 due to low counts following 03/05/2026 cycle. CT imaging from 03/13/2026 demonstrates stable disease when compared to December 2025 scan, though radiology report comparison to September 2025 suggests progression. Weight stabilized at 118 lbs with continued nutritional support including protein supplementation. Potassium levels elevated requiring discontinuation of potassium chloride. Patient remains afebrile with normal bowel function and absence of lower extremity edema.

Plan:
Continue gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab therapy given evidence of disease stability on appropriate imaging comparison to December 2025 scan.
Discontinue potassium chloride supplementation due to elevated potassium levels.
Continue Lasix and spironolactone regimen.
Consider modifying treatment schedule to every 2 weeks instead of current weekly dosing to improve count recovery and manage treatment-related toxicity.
Encourage continued protein supplementation with goal of increasing to 2 supplements daily.
Provide copies of imaging reports from September, December, and current scan for patient review.
Follow up for next treatment cycle pending count recovery.
